prompt.question: In Spring, what is the purpose of the @Primary annotation?
feedbacks: Correct, the @Primary annotation is used to mark a bean as the default candidate for dependency injection when multiple beans of the same type are available. If no other qualifier is specified, the @Primary bean will be injected.
Incorrect, the @Primary annotation does not affect the scope of a bean. Bean scope is controlled using the @Scope annotation.
Incorrect, the @Primary annotation does not control bean initialization timing. Eager initialization is achieved by setting the @Lazy annotation to false or by depending on the bean during startup.
Incorrect, the @Primary annotation does not influence the order of bean initialization. The @Order annotation or Ordered interface is used for ordering beans.
Incorrect, the @Primary annotation does not define the lifecycle or scope of a bean. By default, Spring beans are singleton scoped unless explicitly specified otherwise.
prompt.explanation:
What is @Primary in Spring?
The @Primary annotation is used in Spring to resolve ambiguity when multiple beans of the same type exist in the application context. It designates one bean as the default candidate for dependency injection unless another bean is explicitly specified using @Qualifier.

Key Features of @Primary:

  1. Default Bean Selection:

    • When multiple beans of the same type exist, the @Primary annotation marks one as the default candidate for injection.

    • Example:

      java@Component
public class ServiceA implements MyService {
}

@Component
@Primary
public class ServiceB implements MyService {
}

      • If a MyService dependency is injected without a qualifier, ServiceB will be injected because it is marked with @Primary.

  2. Works with @Qualifier:

    • If a specific bean is required, the @Qualifier annotation can override the @Primary designation.

    • Example:

      java@Autowired
@Qualifier("serviceA")
private MyService myService;

  3. Usage in Configuration Classes:

    • @Primary can also be used on beans defined in @Configuration classes.

    • Example:

      java@Configuration
public class AppConfig {
    @Bean
    public MyService serviceA() {
        return new ServiceA();
    }

    @Bean
    @Primary
    public MyService serviceB() {
        return new ServiceB();
    }
}

  4. Does Not Affect Bean Scope or Initialization:

    • The @Primary annotation only resolves ambiguity during dependency injection. It does not affect the scope (singleton, prototype, etc.) or initialization timing of the bean.

Best Practices for Using @Primary:

  1. Use @Primary for Default Beans:

    • Use @Primary to designate a default bean when multiple beans of the same type exist, especially when one bean is used more frequently than others.

  2. Combine with @Qualifier:

    • Use @Qualifier to inject specific beans when needed, even if one bean is marked as @Primary.

  3. Avoid Overusing @Primary:

    • Overusing @Primary can lead to confusion in large applications. Use it sparingly and document its purpose clearly.

  4. Test Bean Injection:

    • Ensure that the correct bean is injected in all scenarios, especially when using both @Primary and @Qualifier.
answers: It is used to define the default bean to be injected when multiple beans of the same type exist.
It is used to override the default scope of a bean from singleton to prototype.
It is used to ensure that a bean is eagerly initialized during application startup.
It is used to specify the order of bean initialization in the Spring context.
It is used to mark a bean as a singleton in the Spring context.

prompt.question: What are the advantages when using @MockBean instead of @Mock in Spring Boot tests?
feedbacks: Correct, @MockBean is specifically designed to work with Spring Boot test slices such as @WebMvcTest or @DataJpaTest. It integrates seamlessly with these annotations by replacing specific beans in the Spring application context with mocks.
Incorrect, @MockBean still requires Mockito as a dependency since it is built on top of Mockito. It does not eliminate the need for Mockito in your project.

Correct, @MockBean replaces a bean in the Spring application context with a mock. This ensures that all references to the mocked bean in the application context use the mock, making it ideal for integration tests where you want to isolate specific components.
Incorrect, @MockBean is specific to Spring Boot's testing framework and is not compatible with MockitoJUnitRunner. Instead, it works with @SpringBootTest or other Spring Boot test annotations.
prompt.explanation:
What is @MockBean?
@MockBean is an annotation provided by Spring Boot for use in tests. It is used to create and inject Mockito mocks into the Spring application context. This allows you to replace specific beans in the context with mocked instances, making it easier to isolate the component under test.

Advantages of @MockBean Over @Mock:

  1. Integration with Spring Context:

    • @MockBean replaces a real bean in the Spring application context with a mock. This ensures that all references to the bean in the context use the mock, making it ideal for integration tests.

    • Example:

      java@SpringBootTest
public class MyServiceTest {
    @MockBean
    private MyRepository myRepository;

    @Autowired
    private MyService myService;

    @Test
    public void testService() {
        when(myRepository.findById(1L)).thenReturn(Optional.of(new MyEntity()));
        myService.performAction(1L);
        verify(myRepository).findById(1L);
    }
}

  2. Works with Spring Boot Test Slices:

    • @MockBean integrates seamlessly with Spring Boot test slices like @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, and @SpringBootTest. This allows you to test specific layers of the application while mocking dependencies.

    • Example with @WebMvcTest:

      java@WebMvcTest(MyController.class)
public class MyControllerTest {

    @MockBean
    private MyService myService;

    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @Test
    public void testController() throws Exception {
        when(myService.getData()).thenReturn("Mocked Data");

        mockMvc.perform(get("/data"))
               .andExpect(status().isOk())
               .andExpect(content().string("Mocked Data"));
    }
}

  3. Automatic Injection:

    • When you use @MockBean, Spring automatically injects the mock into all places where the real bean would have been injected.

  4. Isolation of Components:

    • By mocking specific beans in the application context, you can isolate the component under test without affecting other components.


Best Practices for Using @MockBean:

  1. Use for Integration Testing:

    • Use @MockBean when testing Spring components that depend on other beans in the application context. It allows you to isolate the component under test.

  2. Combine with Test Slices:

    • Use @MockBean with Spring Boot test slices like @WebMvcTest or @DataJpaTest to test specific layers of the application while mocking dependencies.

  3. Avoid Overusing Mocks:

    • While @MockBean is useful, avoid overusing it in tests. For unit tests, prefer using @Mock and MockitoJUnitRunner.

  4. Verify Mock Interactions:

    • Use Mockito's verify method to ensure that the mocked beans are being used as expected.
answers: Allows integration with Spring Boot test slices like @WebMvcTest or @DataJpaTest.
Mocks all references in the application context to a bean

prompt.question: In Spring AOP, which access specifiers can be intercepted by proxy-based AOP (using @Aspect annotations) by default?
feedbacks: Incorrect, Spring AOP (proxy-based) can only intercept public methods by default. Private and protected methods cannot be intercepted because Spring AOP relies on proxies, which do not have access to private or protected methods.

Correct, Spring AOP (proxy-based) can only intercept public methods by default. This is because Spring AOP uses JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB proxies, and both are limited to intercepting public methods in the context of proxy-based AOP.
Incorrect, Spring AOP (proxy-based) cannot intercept protected methods. It only intercepts public methods by default.

Incorrect, Spring AOP (proxy-based) cannot intercept all methods. Only public methods are intercepted by default. To intercept private or protected methods, AspectJ weaving (compile-time or load-time weaving) is required, which is different from proxy-based AOP.
prompt.explanation:
Spring AOP and Access Specifiers:

  1. Proxy-Based AOP:

    • Spring AOP is proxy-based by default. It uses JDK dynamic proxies (for interfaces) or CGLIB proxies (for classes) to implement aspects.

    • Proxy-based AOP can only intercept public methods because the proxy object wraps the target object and delegates calls to it. Private and protected methods are not accessible to the proxy.

  2. AspectJ Weaving:

    • To intercept private or protected methods, AspectJ weaving (compile-time or load-time weaving) must be used. AspectJ is more powerful than Spring AOP but requires additional configuration and tools.

  3. Access Specifier Limitations:

    • Public Methods: Can be intercepted by Spring AOP (proxy-based).

    • Protected and Private Methods: Cannot be intercepted by Spring AOP (proxy-based). AspectJ is required to handle these cases.

Example:

Proxy-Based AOP Example:

java@Aspect
@Component
public class LoggingAspect {

    @Before("execution(public * com.example.service.*.*(..))")
    public void logBefore(JoinPoint joinPoint) {
        System.out.println("Before method: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
    }
}

  • This aspect will intercept only public methods in the com.example.service package.

  • Private or protected methods in the same package will not be intercepted.

AspectJ Weaving Example (for private methods):

java@Aspect
public class LoggingAspect {

    @Before("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))")
    public void logBefore(JoinPoint joinPoint) {
        System.out.println("Before method: " + joinPoint.getSignature().getName());
    }
}

  • This aspect can intercept all methods (public, protected, private) when using AspectJ weaving. However, this requires additional configuration and tools.

Best Practices for Spring AOP:

  1. Understand Proxy Limitations:

    • Be aware that Spring AOP (proxy-based) can only intercept public methods. If you need to intercept private or protected methods, consider using AspectJ.

  2. Use AOP for Cross-Cutting Concerns:

    • Use AOP for concerns like logging, security, or transaction management. Avoid overusing AOP for business logic.

  3. Keep Aspects Simple:

    • Ensure that aspects are easy to understand and maintain. Avoid complex pointcut expressions unless necessary.

  4. Use AspectJ Only When Needed:

    • Use AspectJ weaving only if you need to intercept private or protected methods or require advanced AOP features. For most use cases, Spring AOP (proxy-based) is sufficient.

prompt.question: Which of these statements are true about the @Autowired annotation in Spring?
feedbacks:
Correct, the @Autowired annotation has a required property (default is true) that specifies whether the dependency is mandatory. If set to false, Spring will not throw an exception if no bean is available for injection.

  • Example:

    @Autowired(required = false)
private MyService myService; // Injection is optional

Incorrect, the method name does not need to start with set. The @Autowired annotation can be applied to any method, regardless of its name, as long as Spring can resolve the dependencies in the method parameters.
Incorrect, a method annotated with @Autowired can have multiple arguments. Spring will resolve and inject all the required dependencies into the method parameters.

Correct, when @Autowired is used on an array field or method argument, Spring will inject all beans of the specified type (MyClass) into the array.

  • Example:

    @Autowired
private MyClass[] myClasses; // All beans of type MyClass will be injected
prompt.explanation:
What is @Autowired in Spring?
The @Autowired annotation is used to enable dependency injection in Spring. It allows Spring to resolve and inject beans automatically based on type. It can be applied to fields, constructors, methods, or method parameters.

Key Features of @Autowired:

  1. Dependency Injection by Type:

    • Spring resolves dependencies by type when using @Autowired. If multiple beans of the same type exist, you can use @Qualifier to specify which bean to inject.

  2. Optional Injection with required:

    • The required property (default is true) specifies whether the dependency is mandatory. If set to false, Spring will inject null if no matching bean is found.

  3. Injection on Arrays or Collections:

    • When @Autowired is used on an array or collection, Spring injects all beans of the specified type into the array or collection.

  4. Method Injection:

    • @Autowired can be applied to methods with any number of arguments. Spring will resolve and inject all the required dependencies into the method parameters.

  5. Constructor Injection:

    • @Autowired can be used on constructors to inject dependencies. If there is only one constructor, the @Autowired annotation is optional.

Examples:

  1. Optional Injection with required:

    java@Autowired(required = false)
private MyService myService; // Will not throw an exception if no bean is found

  2. Method Injection:

    java@Autowired
public void configure(MyService myService, AnotherService anotherService) {
    // Both dependencies will be injected
}

  3. Array Injection:

    java@Autowired
private MyService[] myServices; // All beans of type MyService will be injected

  4. Constructor Injection:

    java@Autowired
public MyController(MyService myService) {
    this.myService = myService; // Dependency is injected via the constructor
}

Best Practices for Using @Autowired:

  1. Prefer Constructor Injection:

    • Constructor injection is preferred over field injection for better testability and immutability.

  2. Use @Qualifier for Ambiguity:

    • If multiple beans of the same type exist, use @Qualifier to specify which bean to inject.

  3. Avoid Overusing Field Injection:

    • Field injection can make testing difficult. Prefer constructor or method injection instead.

  4. Handle Optional Dependencies Gracefully:

    • Use required = false for optional dependencies, but ensure that your code handles null values appropriately.
answers: The Autowired annotation has a ‘required’ property to indicate if the value being injected is optional.
When the Autowired annotation is used on a method, the method name must start with ‘set’.
When the Autowired annotation is used on a method, the method can only have up to 1 argument on the method signature.
When the Autowired annotation is used on an array field or an array method argument ‘MyClass[]’, all beans of type MyClass in the Spring context, will be in the array injected.

prompt.question: In Spring Boot tests, which of the following statements about TestRestTemplate is correct?
feedbacks: Incorrect, TestRestTemplate is not automatically configured in tests with WebEnvironment.NONE because no web server is started in this mode. It is only available when the web environment is set to WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT or WebEnvironment.DEFINED_PORT.
Incorrect, TestRestTemplate is not guaranteed to be thread-safe. Each test should use its own instance to avoid potential issues in concurrent test execution.
Correct, TestRestTemplate follows HTTP redirects by default, making it convenient for testing endpoints that perform redirects (e.g., 302 Found responses). This behavior can be customized if needed.
Incorrect, TestRestTemplate is specifically designed for testing purposes and should not be used in production code. For production, RestTemplate or WebClient should be used.
prompt.explanation:
What is TestRestTemplate?
TestRestTemplate is a utility provided by Spring Boot for testing RESTful web services. It is a simplified version of RestTemplate with additional features tailored for integration testing, such as easy configuration and support for authentication.

Key Features of TestRestTemplate:

  1. Integration with Spring Boot Tests:

    • TestRestTemplate is automatically configured when using @SpringBootTest with WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT or WebEnvironment.DEFINED_PORT. It is not available in WebEnvironment.NONE because no web server is started in this mode.

  2. Automatic Redirect Handling:

    • By default, TestRestTemplate follows HTTP redirects (e.g., 302 Found responses). This behavior is useful for testing endpoints that redirect to other locations.

  3. Simplified Authentication:

    • TestRestTemplate provides methods for basic authentication, making it easy to test secured endpoints.

    • Example:

      javaTestRestTemplate testRestTemplate = new TestRestTemplate("user", "password");

  4. Not Thread-Safe:

    • TestRestTemplate is not guaranteed to be thread-safe. Each test should use its own instance to avoid issues in concurrent test execution.

  5. Testing REST Endpoints:

    • It provides methods for sending HTTP requests and receiving responses, such as getForEntity, postForEntity, and exchange.

Example Usage:

Testing a REST Endpoint with TestRestTemplate:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = SpringBootTest.WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
public class MyControllerTest {

    @Autowired
    private TestRestTemplate testRestTemplate;

    @Test
    public void testGetEndpoint() {
        ResponseEntity<String> response = testRestTemplate.getForEntity("/api/resource", String.class);

        assertEquals(HttpStatus.OK, response.getStatusCode());
        assertEquals("Expected Response", response.getBody());
    }

    @Test
    public void testRedirectEndpoint() {
        ResponseEntity<String> response = testRestTemplate.getForEntity("/api/redirect", String.class);

        assertEquals(HttpStatus.OK, response.getStatusCode());
        assertEquals("Final Destination", response.getBody());
    }
}

Best Practices for Using TestRestTemplate:

  1. Use with WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT:

    • Use TestRestTemplate with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT) to test REST endpoints in an isolated environment.

  2. Avoid Sharing Instances:

    • Do not share TestRestTemplate instances across tests. Each test should use its own instance to avoid concurrency issues.

  3. Test Authentication:

    • Use the built-in support for basic authentication to test secured endpoints.

  4. Verify Redirect Behavior:

    • Take advantage of the default redirect-following behavior to test endpoints that perform redirects.

  5. Use Assertions for Responses:

    • Always assert the status code, headers, and body of the response to ensure the endpoint behaves as expected.
answers: TestRestTemplate is automatically configured and injected into tests annotated with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.NONE).
TestRestTemplate is thread-safe and can be shared across multiple test cases.
TestRestTemplate is a replacement for RestTemplate in production applications.

}

Incorrect, @ContextConfiguration is not limited to @SpringBootTest. It can be used with other test annotations like @RunWith(SpringJUnit4ClassRunner.class) or @ExtendWith(SpringExtension.class) for non-Spring Boot tests.

Correct, @ContextConfiguration can be used alongside @ActiveProfiles to load configurations specific to a particular Spring profile.

  • Example:

    @ActiveProfiles("test")
@ContextConfiguration(classes = AppConfig.class)
public class MyTest {
}

Incorrect, @ContextConfiguration does not perform component scanning by itself. To enable component scanning, you need to explicitly define it in the configuration class using @ComponentScan or in XML configuration.
prompt.explanation:
What is @ContextConfiguration?
@ContextConfiguration is an annotation in Spring used to specify how the application context should be loaded for a test. It is commonly used in integration tests to define the configuration classes or XML files that should be used to initialize the Spring container.

Key Features of @ContextConfiguration:

  1. Loading Configuration Classes:

    • You can specify one or more Java configuration classes to load the application context.

    • Example:

      @ContextConfiguration(classes = AppConfig.class)
public class MyTest {
}

  2. Loading XML Configuration:

    • You can specify the location of XML configuration files to load the application context.

    • Example:

      @ContextConfiguration(locations = "classpath:app-config.xml")

  3. Combining with Profiles:

    • You can use @ActiveProfiles with @ContextConfiguration to load profile-specific configurations.

  4. Flexible Usage:

    • @ContextConfiguration can be used with various testing frameworks, such as JUnit 4 (@RunWith(SpringJUnit4ClassRunner.class)) or JUnit 5 (@ExtendWith(SpringExtension.class)).

  5. Customizing Context Initialization:

    • You can customize how the context is initialized by providing a custom ContextLoader.

Example Usage:

  1. Loading Multiple Configuration Classes:

    @RunWith(SpringJUnit4ClassRunner.class)
@ContextConfiguration(classes = {AppConfig.class, SecurityConfig.class})
public class MyTest {
    @Autowired
    private MyService myService;

    @Test
    public void testService() {
        assertNotNull(myService);
    }
}

  2. Loading XML Configuration:

    @RunWith(SpringJUnit4ClassRunner.class)
@ContextConfiguration(locations = "classpath:app-config.xml")
public class MyTest {
    @Autowired
    private MyService myService;

    @Test
    public void testService() {
        assertNotNull(myService);
    }
}

  3. Using with Profiles:

    @RunWith(SpringJUnit4ClassRunner.class)
@ActiveProfiles("test")
@ContextConfiguration(classes = AppConfig.class)
public class MyTest {
    @Autowired
    private MyService myService;

    @Test
    public void testService() {
        assertNotNull(myService);
    }
}

Best Practices for Using @ContextConfiguration:

  1. Use Java Config When Possible:

    • Prefer Java configuration classes over XML for better maintainability and type safety.

  2. Combine with Profiles:

    • Use @ActiveProfiles to load profile-specific configurations for tests.

  3. Keep Tests Isolated:

    • Ensure that each test loads only the necessary configurations to avoid unnecessary dependencies and speed up test execution.

  4. Use @SpringBootTest for Spring Boot Applications:

    • For Spring Boot applications, prefer @SpringBootTest over @ContextConfiguration unless you need fine-grained control over the context initialization.
answers: @ContextConfiguration can be used to load multiple configuration classes for a Spring test.
@ContextConfiguration can load XML configuration files into the application context.
@ContextConfiguration can be combined with @ActiveProfiles to load profile-specific configurations.
@ContextConfiguration automatically scans for components in the base package.

prompt.question: Spring Security provides mechanisms to prevent CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) attacks. Is this statement true or false?
feedbacks: Correct, Spring Security provides built-in support to prevent CSRF attacks. By default, CSRF protection is enabled in Spring Security for web applications. It ensures that state-changing requests (e.g., POST, PUT, DELETE) are accompanied by a valid CSRF token, which is verified on the server side to prevent unauthorized actions.

Incorrect, Spring Security includes mechanisms to prevent CSRF attacks. Disabling CSRF protection is possible but not recommended unless the application is designed in a way that does not require it (e.g., stateless REST APIs secured with tokens).
prompt.explanation:
What is CSRF?
CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) is a type of attack where a malicious website tricks a user into performing an unintended action on a trusted website where the user is authenticated. For example, an attacker could trick a user into submitting a form that transfers funds or changes account settings without their knowledge.

How Spring Security Prevents CSRF Attacks:

  1. CSRF Token:

    • Spring Security generates a unique CSRF token for each user session.

    • The token is included in the HTML forms as a hidden field or in a custom HTTP header for AJAX requests.

    • Example of a CSRF token in a form:

      html<input type="hidden" name="_csrf" value="generated-csrf-token">

  2. Token Validation:

    • When a state-changing request (e.g., POST, PUT, DELETE) is sent to the server, Spring Security validates the CSRF token.

    • If the token is missing or invalid, the request is rejected with a 403 Forbidden response.

  3. Default Behavior:

    • CSRF protection is enabled by default in Spring Security for web applications.

    • It applies to state-changing HTTP methods (e.g., POST, PUT, DELETE) but not to safe methods like GET, HEAD, or OPTIONS.

  4. Customizing CSRF Protection:

    • CSRF protection can be customized or disabled in the SecurityFilterChain configuration.

    • Example of disabling CSRF protection:

      java@Configuration
public class SecurityConfig {
    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain securityFilterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http.csrf().disable(); // Disables CSRF protection
        return http.build();
    }
}

Best Practices for CSRF Protection:

  1. Keep CSRF Protection Enabled for Web Applications:

    • Always enable CSRF protection for traditional web applications that use cookies for session management.

  2. Use Tokens for Stateless APIs:

    • For REST APIs, consider using stateless authentication mechanisms like JWT (JSON Web Tokens) or OAuth2 tokens instead of CSRF protection.

  3. Include CSRF Tokens in AJAX Requests:

    • For AJAX requests, include the CSRF token in a custom HTTP header.

    • Example:

      javascriptconst csrfToken = document.querySelector('meta[name="_csrf"]').getAttribute('content');
fetch('/api/resource', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
        'X-CSRF-TOKEN': csrfToken
    },
    body: JSON.stringify(data)
});

results.question_plain: Spring Security provides mechanisms to prevent CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) attacks. Is this statement true or false?

prompt.question: In Spring Security Java-based configuration, which method can be used to bypass the filter chain entirely for specific URL patterns?
feedbacks: Incorrect. There is no such method as ignoreAll() in Spring Security. This is a made-up or invalid API.
Incorrect, there is no such method as filtersNone() in Spring Security's Java-based configuration. This is an invalid option.

Incorrect, this method allows anonymous users to access the specified URL patterns, but it does not bypass the filter chain. The filter chain remains active, and the request is processed as an anonymous user.

Correct, the security.ignoring() method is used in Spring Security Java-based configuration to completely bypass the filter chain for the specified URL patterns. This disables all Spring Security features for those URLs.

  • Example:

    @Configuration
public class SecurityConfig extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter {
    @Override
    public void configure(WebSecurity web) throws Exception {
        web.ignoring().antMatchers("/public/**");
    }
}
prompt.explanation:
What is WebSecurity.ignoring() in Spring Security?
In Spring Security, the web.ignoring() method is used to exclude specific URL patterns from the Spring Security filter chain entirely. This means that requests matching these patterns will not be processed by any Spring Security filters, effectively disabling all security features for those requests.

Key Differences Between web.ignoring() and permitAll():

  1. web.ignoring():

    • Completely bypasses the Spring Security filter chain for the specified URL patterns.

    • No security features (e.g., authentication, authorization, CSRF protection) are applied to the matching requests.

    • Example:

      java@Override
public void configure(WebSecurity web) throws Exception {
    web.ignoring().antMatchers("/public/**");
}

  2. permitAll():

    • Allows unauthenticated access to the specified URL patterns but does not bypass the filter chain.

    • The filter chain is still active, and other security features (e.g., CSRF protection) may still apply.

    • Example:

      java@Override
protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    http.authorizeRequests()
        .antMatchers("/public/**").permitAll();
}

Example Usage:

Bypassing the Filter Chain with web.ignoring():

java@Configuration
public class SecurityConfig extends WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter {

    @Override
    public void configure(WebSecurity web) throws Exception {
        // Completely bypass the filter chain for /public/** URLs
        web.ignoring().antMatchers("/public/**");
    }

    @Override
    protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        http.authorizeRequests()
            .antMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN") // Secure admin URLs
            .anyRequest().authenticated(); // Secure all other URLs
    }
}

  • In this example:

    • Requests to /public/** bypass the Spring Security filter chain entirely.

    • Requests to /admin/** require the user to have the ADMIN role.

    • All other requests require authentication.

Best Practices for Using web.ignoring():

  1. Use with Caution:

    • Avoid using web.ignoring() unless absolutely necessary. Completely bypassing the filter chain disables all security features, leaving the URLs unprotected.

  2. Secure Public Resources Separately:

    • If you need to allow unauthenticated access to certain resources (e.g., static files), consider configuring them in the web server (e.g., Tomcat, Nginx) instead of bypassing the filter chain.

  3. Prefer permitAll() for Most Cases:

    • Use permitAll() instead of web.ignoring() for public URLs that still require some security features (e.g., CSRF protection).

results.question_plain: In Spring Security Java-based configuration, which method can be used to bypass the filter chain entirely for specific URL patterns?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are correct, regarding CGLIB Proxy?
feedbacks: Correct, CGLIB proxies, like JDK dynamic proxies, do not support self-invocation. If a method in the same class calls another proxied method, the proxy is bypassed, and the call is made directly to the target method.
Incorrect, CGLIB proxies do not intercept private methods. Only public and protected methods can be proxied by CGLIB. Private methods are not part of the proxy mechanism.
Incorrect, if a class implements an interface, Spring will use JDK dynamic proxies by default. CGLIB proxies are used only when no interfaces are implemented or when explicitly configured.
Correct, CGLIB proxies cannot proxy final methods because it relies on subclassing the target class, and final methods cannot be overridden in subclasses.
prompt.explanation:
What is CGLIB Proxy?
CGLIB (Code Generation Library) is a library used by Spring for creating proxies of classes. Unlike JDK dynamic proxies, which require an interface, CGLIB proxies work by subclassing the target class. This allows proxying of classes without interfaces, but it comes with certain limitations.

Key Features of CGLIB Proxy:

  1. Subclassing Mechanism:

    • CGLIB creates a subclass of the target class at runtime and overrides its methods to add proxy behavior.

  2. No Interface Required:

    • Unlike JDK dynamic proxies, CGLIB proxies do not require the target class to implement an interface.

  3. Limitations:

    • CGLIB proxies cannot intercept final methods because final methods cannot be overridden in subclasses.

    • CGLIB proxies do not intercept private methods because they are not accessible to subclasses.

  4. Self-Invocation Limitation:

    • Like JDK dynamic proxies, CGLIB proxies do not support self-invocation. When a method in the same class calls another proxied method, the proxy is bypassed, and the call is made directly to the target method.

  5. Default Behavior in Spring:

    • Spring uses JDK dynamic proxies by default if the target class implements at least one interface.

    • CGLIB proxies are used if the target class does not implement any interfaces or if explicitly configured.

Example:

CGLIB Proxy in Action:

@Configuration
@EnableAspectJAutoProxy(proxyTargetClass = true) // Forces the use of CGLIB proxies
public class AppConfig {
}

@Component
public class MyService {
    public void publicMethod() {
        System.out.println("Public method");
        privateMethod(); // Self-invocation bypasses proxy
    }

    private void privateMethod() {
        System.out.println("Private method");
    }

    public final void finalMethod() {
        System.out.println("Final method");
    }
}

  • Self-Invocation Limitation:
    When publicMethod() calls privateMethod(), the proxy is bypassed, and the call is made directly to the target method.

  • Final Method Limitation:
    The finalMethod() cannot be proxied because it is declared as final.

Best Practices for Using CGLIB Proxies:

  1. Use JDK Proxies When Possible:

    • Prefer JDK dynamic proxies if the target class implements interfaces, as they are simpler and have fewer limitations.

  2. Avoid Final Methods in Target Classes:

    • Avoid declaring methods as final if they need to be proxied.

  3. Be Aware of Self-Invocation:

    • Avoid self-invocation of proxied methods. If necessary, refactor the code to call the method from another bean.

  4. Explicitly Configure Proxy Mechanism:

    • Use the @EnableAspectJAutoProxy(proxyTargetClass = true) annotation or proxyTargetClass property in XML to force the use of CGLIB proxies if needed.
answers: CGLIB Proxy works with private methods
CGLIB Proxy will be used by default if a class implements an interface
CGLIB Proxy doesn't work with final methods

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements are correct, regarding CGLIB Proxy?

prompt.question: The annotation _______ is used on test-classes testing only Spring MVC components while using Spring Boot
feedbacks: Incorrect, there is no such annotation as @MvcTest in Spring Boot. The correct annotation for testing Spring MVC components is @WebMvcTest.
Incorrect, @WebTest is not a valid Spring Boot annotation. It does not exist in the Spring framework.

Correct, @WebMvcTest is the annotation used to test Spring MVC components such as controllers. It configures the application context with only the beans required for Spring MVC testing, excluding other layers like services or repositories.
Incorrect, @Test is a JUnit annotation used for marking test methods, but it does not configure the application context for testing Spring MVC components.
prompt.explanation:
What is @WebMvcTest?
@WebMvcTest is a specialized Spring Boot test annotation used to test Spring MVC components, such as controllers. It sets up a minimal application context that includes only the MVC-related beans, such as @Controller, @RestController, @ControllerAdvice, and @JsonComponent. Other beans, such as services or repositories, are not loaded unless explicitly mocked or included.

Key Features of @WebMvcTest:

  1. Focused Testing:

    • @WebMvcTest is used for testing the web layer (controllers) in isolation from other layers, such as the service or data layers.

  2. Minimal Application Context:

    • It loads only the Spring MVC components required for testing.

    • Example:

      • Controllers (@Controller, @RestController)

      • Exception handlers (@ControllerAdvice)

      • JSON serializers/deserializers (@JsonComponent)

  3. Mocking Dependencies:

    • Dependencies like services or repositories need to be mocked using @MockBean or similar mechanisms since they are not loaded into the application context.

  4. Integration with MockMvc:

    • @WebMvcTest automatically configures a MockMvc instance for testing HTTP requests and responses.

Testing a Spring Controller with @WebMvcTest:

@WebMvcTest(MyController.class) // Load only MyController and related MVC components
public class MyControllerTest {

    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @MockBean
    private MyService myService; // Mock the service layer

    @Test
    public void testGetEndpoint() throws Exception {
        // Mock service behavior
        when(myService.getData()).thenReturn("Mocked Data");

        // Perform GET request and verify response
        mockMvc.perform(get("/api/data"))
               .andExpect(status().isOk())
               .andExpect(content().string("Mocked Data"));
    }
}

  • Key Points in the Example:

    • @WebMvcTest(MyController.class) loads only the MyController and related Spring MVC components.

    • @MockBean is used to mock the MyService dependency.

    • MockMvc is used to perform HTTP requests and verify responses.

Best Practices for Using @WebMvcTest:

  1. Test Controllers in Isolation:

    • Use @WebMvcTest to test only the web layer without loading the full application context.

  2. Mock Dependencies:

    • Use @MockBean to mock service or repository dependencies that are not loaded by @WebMvcTest.

  3. Use MockMvc for HTTP Testing:

    • Leverage MockMvc to perform HTTP requests and validate responses.

  4. Avoid Loading Unnecessary Beans:

    • Specify the controller to test in the @WebMvcTest annotation to avoid loading unnecessary controllers or beans.

results.question_plain: The annotation _______ is used on test-classes testing only Spring MVC components while using Spring Boot

prompt.question: Which of the following statements accurately defines the purpose of a unit test in software development?
feedbacks: Correct, a unit test focuses on testing a small, isolated unit of functionality (e.g., a single method or class) without relying on the behavior of external components or systems. It ensures that the unit behaves as expected under various conditions.
Incorrect, this describes an integration test, not a unit test. Integration tests focus on verifying the interactions between multiple components or systems.
Incorrect, this describes system testing, which validates the application as a whole in a real-world scenario.
Incorrect, exploratory testing is a manual testing approach where testers explore the application to identify issues. It is not related to unit testing.
prompt.explanation:
What is a Unit Test?
A unit test is a type of automated test that verifies the behavior of a small, isolated piece of code (such as a method or class). The goal of a unit test is to ensure that the code behaves as expected under a variety of conditions, including edge cases. Unit tests are typically written by developers and are executed frequently during development to catch bugs early.

Key Characteristics of Unit Tests:

  1. Isolation:

    • Unit tests focus on testing a single unit of functionality in isolation. Dependencies on other components or systems are replaced with mocks or stubs.

  2. Fast Execution:

    • Unit tests are lightweight and execute quickly, making them suitable for frequent execution during development.

  3. Specific Scope:

    • Each unit test is designed to test a specific piece of functionality, such as a method or class.

  4. Automated:

    • Unit tests are typically automated and integrated into the development workflow using tools like JUnit or TestNG.

  5. Repeatable:

    • Unit tests should produce consistent results when run multiple times under the same conditions.

Testing a Method in Isolation:

public class Calculator {
    public int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }
}

public class CalculatorTest {

    @Test
    public void testAdd() {
        Calculator calculator = new Calculator();
        int result = calculator.add(2, 3);
        assertEquals(5, result); // Verify that 2 + 3 = 5
    }
}

  • What This Unit Test Does:

    • It tests the add method of the Calculator class in isolation.

    • No external dependencies are involved, ensuring the test focuses solely on the behavior of the add method.

Best Practices for Unit Testing:

  1. Keep Tests Isolated:

    • Use mocks or stubs to isolate the unit being tested from its dependencies.

  2. Write Tests for Edge Cases:

    • Ensure that your unit tests cover edge cases and unexpected inputs.

  3. Make Tests Repeatable:

    • Ensure that unit tests produce consistent results when run multiple times under the same conditions.

  4. Run Tests Frequently:

    • Integrate unit tests into your CI/CD pipeline to catch issues early in the development process.

  5. Follow the AAA Pattern:

    • Structure your tests using the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern:

      • Arrange: Set up the test data and environment.

      • Act: Call the method being tested.

      • Assert: Verify that the method behaves as expected.
answers: A unit test verifies the behavior of an individual piece of code, such as a method or class, in isolation from other components.
A unit test is used to validate the integration of multiple components working together.
A unit test validates the overall functionality of the application in a production-like environment.
A unit test is a type of exploratory testing used to investigate specific system behaviors.

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements accurately defines the purpose of a unit test in software development?

prompt.question: What will happen if JdbcTemplate.queryForObject() is executed with a query that does not return any results?
links: [Using the JDBC Core Classes to Control Basic JDBC Processing and Error Handling](https://docs.spring.io/spring-framework/reference/data-access/jdbc/core.html)
feedbacks: queryForObject() does not return null when no rows are found. Instead, it throws an exception.
If the query does not return any results, Spring throws EmptyResultDataAccessException. To avoid this, a try-catch block should be used.
queryForObject() does not return an empty instance of the expected type.
A SQLException is not thrown by queryForObject(), as Spring wraps database exceptions in Spring-specific exceptions like DataAccessException.
prompt.explanation:
Spring JdbcTemplate queryForObject() – Practical Guide

Introduction

Spring’s JdbcTemplate.queryForObject() retrieves a single row from a database and maps it to a Java object. It's useful when you expect exactly one result, but it must be used carefully to handle errors properly.

How queryForObject() Works

Method Signature

public <T> T queryForObject(String sql, Object[] args, RowMapper<T> rowMapper)

  • sql – SQL query with ? placeholders.

  • args – Values for query parameters.

  • rowMapper – Converts database rows into Java objects.

Example Usage

public Account getAccountById(int id) {
    String sql = "SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE id = ?";
    return jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(sql, new Object[]{id}, new AccountMapper());
}

private static class AccountMapper implements RowMapper<Account> {
    @Override
    public Account mapRow(ResultSet rs, int rowNum) throws SQLException {
        return new Account(rs.getInt("id"), rs.getString("name"));
    }
}

  • Retrieves one Account from the database.

  • Uses RowMapper<Account> to map the result set to an object.

Handling Errors

1. No Rows Found → EmptyResultDataAccessException

If no matching record is found, Spring throws an exception instead of returning null.

Solution: Handle with Try-Catch

try {
    return jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(sql, new Object[]{id}, new AccountMapper());
} catch (EmptyResultDataAccessException e) {
    return null; // Handle missing record safely
}

2. More Than One Row Found → IncorrectResultSizeDataAccessException

If the query unexpectedly returns multiple rows, queryForObject() fails.

Solution: Use query() Instead

public List<Account> getAccountsByName(String name) {
    String sql = "SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE name = ?";
    return jdbcTemplate.query(sql, new Object[]{name}, new AccountMapper());
}

Best Practices

  1. Use queryForObject() only when expecting a single row.

  2. Handle EmptyResultDataAccessException to avoid crashes.

  3. Use query() when multiple rows might be returned.

  4. Ensure column names in RowMapper match the database schema.

  5. Use queryForObject(sql, Class<T>) for single-column queries.

Example (Fetching Single Column Value)

tpublic String getAccountNameById(int id) {
    String sql = "SELECT name FROM accounts WHERE id = ?";
    return jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(sql, new Object[]{id}, String.class);
}

Conclusion

  • queryForObject() is efficient for single-record queries but must be used cautiously.

  • Always handle empty results and use query() for multiple rows.

  • Follow best practices to avoid unexpected exceptions and improve database interaction.
answers: It will return null without throwing an exception.
It will return an empty object of the expected type.

results.question_plain: What will happen if JdbcTemplate.queryForObject() is executed with a query that does not return any results?

prompt.question: ____________ testing refers to automated tests for several modules and the interactions between them when they are put together.
links: [Baeldung - Guide to Integration Testing in Spring Boot](https://www.baeldung.com/integration-testing-in-spring)
feedbacks: Focuses on evaluating the system's responsiveness, speed, scalability, and stability under a particular workload. It does not primarily address the interactions between modules.
Not a standard term in software testing. The intended concept is likely covered under integration testing.​
Involves testing individual components or functions of a software application in isolation to ensure they work as intended. It does not assess the interactions between multiple modules.

Involves combining multiple modules and testing them as a group to ensure they work together correctly. This process focuses on detecting interface issues and verifying that different components interact as intended.
prompt.explanation:
Integration Testing in Spring Boot – Practical Guide

1. Introduction

Integration testing in Spring Boot verifies the interactions between multiple components, such as services, repositories, controllers, and external dependencies. Unlike unit tests, which isolate individual methods or classes, integration tests ensure that all parts of the application work together as expected.

Spring Boot provides built-in support for integration testing using:

  • @SpringBootTest for bootstrapping the full application context.

  • TestRestTemplate or MockMvc for testing web endpoints.

  • @Transactional for database rollback after each test.

  • Embedded databases (H2, HSQLDB) for isolated database testing.

2. Setting Up an Integration Test in Spring Boot

2.1 Adding Dependencies

For integration testing, you need to include the Spring Boot test starter:

<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>
    <scope>test</scope>
</dependency>

This provides JUnit, Spring Test, and Mockito.

If you use Spring Data JPA, also add:

xml
CopyEdit<dependency>
    <groupId>com.h2database</groupId>
    <artifactId>h2</artifactId>
    <scope>test</scope>
</dependency>

H2 is an in-memory database useful for testing data persistence.

2.2 Writing an Integration Test Using @SpringBootTest

The @SpringBootTest annotation loads the entire Spring Boot application context.

@SpringBootTest
@RunWith(SpringRunner.class)
public class UserServiceIntegrationTest {

    @Autowired
    private UserService userService;

    @Test
    public void whenCreateUser_thenUserIsSaved() {
        User user = new User("John", "john@example.com");
        User savedUser = userService.createUser(user);

        assertNotNull(savedUser.getId());
        assertEquals("John", savedUser.getName());
    }
}

Key Features:

  • @SpringBootTest loads the full application context.

  • @Autowired injects dependencies like real application behavior.

  • A real database can be used (or mocked with H2 for faster testing).

3. Testing REST APIs Using TestRestTemplate

Spring Boot provides TestRestTemplate for calling REST endpoints just like a real HTTP client.

java
CopyEdit@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = SpringBootTest.WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
@RunWith(SpringRunner.class)
public class UserControllerIntegrationTest {

    @Autowired
    private TestRestTemplate restTemplate;

    @Test
    public void whenGetUserById_thenReturnUser() {
        ResponseEntity<User> response = restTemplate.getForEntity("/users/1", User.class);

        assertEquals(HttpStatus.OK, response.getStatusCode());
        assertNotNull(response.getBody());
        assertEquals(1, response.getBody().getId());
    }
}

Key Features:

  • webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT starts the application on a random port.

  • TestRestTemplate makes actual HTTP calls to test real API behavior.

4. Mocking Web Requests Using MockMvc

For isolated controller testing, use MockMvc:

@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)
@RunWith(SpringRunner.class)
public class UserControllerTest {

    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;

    @MockBean
    private UserService userService;

    @Test
    public void whenGetUser_thenReturnsUserJson() throws Exception {
        User user = new User(1L, "John", "john@example.com");
        Mockito.when(userService.getUserById(1L)).thenReturn(user);

        mockMvc.perform(get("/users/1"))
                .andExpect(status().isOk())
                .andExpect(jsonPath("$.name").value("John"));
    }
}

Key Features:

  • @WebMvcTest(UserController.class) loads only the controller layer.

  • MockMvc performs simulated HTTP requests without needing an actual web server.

  • @MockBean replaces actual beans with Mockito mocks.

5. Database Integration Testing with @DataJpaTest

Spring Boot provides @DataJpaTest to test only the JPA layer.

@DataJpaTest
@RunWith(SpringRunner.class)
public class UserRepositoryTest {

    @Autowired
    private UserRepository userRepository;

    @Test
    public void whenSaveUser_thenFindById() {
        User user = new User("John", "john@example.com");
        User savedUser = userRepository.save(user);

        Optional<User> foundUser = userRepository.findById(savedUser.getId());
        assertTrue(foundUser.isPresent());
        assertEquals("John", foundUser.get().getName());
    }
}

Key Features:

  • @DataJpaTest loads only JPA-related beans.

  • Uses an in-memory H2 database by default.

  • Transactions are automatically rolled back after each test.

6. Cleaning Up the Database Between Tests

If you're using a real database, data persists between test runs. To reset data between tests, use:

  • @Transactional – Rolls back database changes after each test.

  • @BeforeEach + userRepository.deleteAll() – Clears data before each test.

Example:

@BeforeEach
public void setup() {
    userRepository.deleteAll();
}

7. Best Practices for Integration Testing

✅ Use @SpringBootTest for full application testing.
✅ Use TestRestTemplate for real HTTP calls.
✅ Use MockMvc for isolated controller tests.
✅ Use @DataJpaTest for testing repositories.
✅ Use an in-memory database (H2) for faster tests.
✅ Clean up the database between tests using @Transactional.

results.question_plain: ____________ testing refers to automated tests for several modules and the interactions between them when they are put together.

prompt.question: It is described as a stage during the execution of a program, such as the execution of a method or the handling of an exception. In Spring AOP, it always represents a method execution. Select the one that applies best.
feedbacks: An Aspect in Spring AOP is a modularization of concerns such as logging, security, or transactions. It defines behavior that is applied to multiple parts of an application. While an aspect contains join points, it is not itself a stage of execution.

A join point represents a specific point in the execution of a program, such as the invocation of a method or handling an exception. In Spring AOP, a join point always refers to a method execution. This makes it the most appropriate answer.
Advice is the actual action (code) executed before, after, or around a join point. While advice is applied at join points, it does not represent an execution stage itself.
A pointcut is an expression that defines which join points should be intercepted. It does not represent an actual stage in the execution flow but rather a selection of execution points.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to AOP Concepts in Spring

1. What is Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP)?

Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) allows developers to separate cross-cutting concerns (like logging, security, or transaction management) from business logic. Instead of duplicating the same code across multiple classes, AOP applies behaviors dynamically at runtime.

2. Key AOP Concepts:

ConceptDescriptionAspectA module that defines cross-cutting concerns (e.g., logging, transactions).Join PointA specific point in a program's execution (e.g., method call, exception handling).AdviceThe action taken at a join point (e.g., before, after, around a method execution).PointcutA predicate that matches join points (e.g., "apply logging to all methods in the service layer").Target ObjectThe object whose method is being advised (also called the proxied object).

3. Example: Applying Logging with AOP

Here’s how Spring AOP works in practice:

Step 1: Define an Aspect

@Aspect
@Component
public class LoggingAspect {

    @Before("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))")
    public void logBeforeMethodExecution(JoinPoint joinPoint) {
        System.out.println("Executing method: " + joinPoint.getSignature());
    }
}

  • @Aspect → Declares this class as an aspect.

  • @Before("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))") → Defines a pointcut that applies to all methods in the com.example.service package.

  • JoinPoint joinPoint → Represents the method execution, providing details like method name and arguments.

Step 2: Target Service Class

@Service
public class UserService {
    public void createUser(String name) {
        System.out.println("User created: " + name);
    }
}

Step 3: Running the Application

Calling createUser("Alice") will produce:

Executing method: void com.example.service.UserService.createUser(String)
User created: Alice

  • The join point occurs at createUser() execution.

  • The advice logs method details before execution.

4. Common Pointcut Expressions

ExpressionDescriptionexecution(* com.example..*(..))Matches all methods in com.example package.execution(* com.example.service.UserService.*(..))Matches all methods in UserService class.within(com.example.service..*)Matches all methods inside service package.@annotation(org.springframework.transaction.annotation.Transactional)Matches methods annotated with @Transactional.

5. Best Practices for AOP in Spring

✅ Use AOP for cross-cutting concerns like logging, security, transactions.
✅ Keep pointcuts modular by defining them in separate methods inside the aspect class.
✅ Avoid using AOP for business logic – it should only modify behavior, not define it.
✅ Always test pointcuts carefully to avoid unwanted method interceptions.

prompt.question: Which of the following methods in JdbcTemplate is recommended for UPDATE or INSERT operations?
feedbacks:
update (Correct)
The update() method in JdbcTemplate is designed for modifying data in the database, making it the correct choice for INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations.

Example Usage:

String sql = "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)";
int rowsAffected = jdbcTemplate.update(sql, "John Doe", "john@example.com");
System.out.println("Rows inserted: " + rowsAffected);

  • Returns the number of rows affected.

  • Best suited for data modification queries.

This method retrieves multiple rows from the database and returns them as a List<Map<String, Object>>. It is not used for insert or update operations.

Example:

List<Map<String, Object>> users = jdbcTemplate.queryForList("SELECT * FROM users");

The query() method is used to retrieve data from the database and map rows to Java objects using a RowMapper. It is not intended for updates or inserts.

Example:

List<User> users = jdbcTemplate.query("SELECT * FROM users", new UserRowMapper());

The execute() method is primarily used for executing SQL DDL (e.g., creating tables, stored procedures), not for updating or inserting data.

Example:

jdbcTemplate.execute("CREATE TABLE users (id SERIAL, name VARCHAR(255))");
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Modifying Data in Spring JdbcTemplate

1. Using update() for INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE

The update() method is the most commonly used for modifying data.

Inserting Data

String sql = "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)";
jdbcTemplate.update(sql, "Alice", "alice@example.com");

Updating Data

String sql = "UPDATE users SET email = ? WHERE name = ?";
jdbcTemplate.update(sql, "alice.new@example.com", "Alice");

Deleting Data

String sql = "DELETE FROM users WHERE name = ?";
jdbcTemplate.update(sql, "Alice");

2. Using Batch Updates

To improve performance when inserting/updating multiple records, use batch updates:

String sql = "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)";
List<Object[]> batchArgs = List.of(
    new Object[]{"Alice", "alice@example.com"},
    new Object[]{"Bob", "bob@example.com"}
);

jdbcTemplate.batchUpdate(sql, batchArgs);

3. When to Use execute()?

Use execute() for schema operations like table creation:

jdbcTemplate.execute("CREATE TABLE users (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(255))");

Best Practices for Using JdbcTemplate.update()

✅ Use parameterized queries (?) to prevent SQL injection.
✅ Handle exceptions properly (e.g., DataAccessException).
✅ Use batch updates for bulk inserts to improve performance.
✅ Ensure proper transaction management when executing multiple operations.

prompt.question: Which one is true about scopes?
feedbacks:
The singleton scope in Spring means that the bean is created at application startup (eager initialization by default), not lazily upon request.

  • However, lazy initialization can be explicitly configured using @Lazy.

@Bean
@Lazy
public MyBean myBean() {
    return new MyBean();
}

  • Without @Lazy, the bean is instantiated at startup.

In prototype scope, Spring does not manage the full lifecycle of a bean.

  • Only the initialization callbacks (@PostConstruct, InitializingBean) are executed.

  • Destruction callbacks (@PreDestroy, DisposableBean) are NOT called.

  • If you need cleanup for prototype beans, the client code must manually invoke the cleanup method.

The opposite is true:

  • Singleton should be used for stateless beans because they are shared across the application.

  • Prototype should be used for stateful beans when each request needs a new instance.
Since A and C are incorrect, "All of the above" is also incorrect.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Bean Scopes in Spring

What Are Bean Scopes?

Spring provides different bean scopes to define how long a bean exists and how it is managed within the application context.

Common Bean Scopes in Spring

  • Singleton (default scope) ensures that only one instance of the bean exists in the Spring container. It is created at startup and shared for all requests.

  • Prototype creates a new instance of the bean every time it is requested. Spring does not manage its lifecycle beyond initialization.

  • Request (Web Scope) ensures a new instance is created for each HTTP request.

  • Session (Web Scope) maintains a bean instance for the duration of a user session.

  • Application (Web Scope) creates a single instance per ServletContext.

Singleton vs. Prototype Scope

A singleton bean is useful for stateless components such as services, repositories, and controllers, as they can be reused without side effects. A prototype bean is useful for stateful objects, such as UI components or objects with user-specific configurations, where a new instance is needed for each operation.

Lifecycle Callbacks in Singleton vs. Prototype

A singleton bean follows the full Spring lifecycle, including initialization and destruction callbacks. A prototype bean, however, only calls initialization callbacks like @PostConstruct, while destruction callbacks like @PreDestroy are ignored. This means that if a prototype bean has cleanup logic, it must be explicitly managed by the developer.

For example, a singleton bean with lifecycle callbacks:

@Component
public class SingletonBean {
    @PostConstruct
    public void init() {
        System.out.println("Singleton initialized");
    }

    @PreDestroy
    public void destroy() {
        System.out.println("Singleton destroyed");
    }
}

A prototype bean requires manual cleanup:

@Component
@Scope("prototype")
public class PrototypeBean {
    @PostConstruct
    public void init() {
        System.out.println("Prototype initialized");
    }

    @PreDestroy
    public void destroy() {
        System.out.println("Prototype destroyed"); // Will NOT be called automatically
    }
}

To ensure the cleanup method runs for a prototype bean, the developer must manually destroy it:

PrototypeBean prototype = applicationContext.getBean(PrototypeBean.class);
// Perform operations
((ConfigurableApplicationContext) applicationContext).getBeanFactory().destroyBean(prototype);

Best Practices for Managing Bean Scopes

  • Use singleton scope for stateless beans such as service and repository components.

  • Use prototype scope for stateful beans where a new instance is required for every request.

  • Be aware that Spring does not manage the destruction of prototype beans, so cleanup must be handled manually.

  • When using request, session, or application scopes, ensure the application runs within a web environment, as these scopes rely on HTTP request handling.
answers: By default, a ‘singleton’ is initialized only when a client requests it, and stored in a cache for all subsequent requests to use.
Configured destruction lifecycle callbacks on prototypes are not called.
Ideally, use the prototype scope for all stateless beans and the singleton scope for stateful beans.

results.question_plain: Which one is true about scopes?

prompt.question: Which annotation can be used to inject the Spring Boot Actuator port value in a field used in a Spring Boot class?
feedbacks: There is no such annotation in Spring Boot.

The @Value annotation is used to inject property values into fields. When using @Value("${local.management.port}"), the field is injected with the dynamically assigned management HTTP port, where Actuator endpoints are exposed.

@Value("${local.management.port}")
private int managementPort;

This approach

Spring Boot provides @LocalManagementPort to directly inject the management server port. This is a more convenient and type-safe way to access the management port compared to @Value("${local.management.port}").

@LocalManagementPort
private int managementPort;

The @LocalManagementPort annotation is available in the org.springframework.boot.actuate.autoconfigure.web.server package.
This is the preferred method in Spring Boot 2.x+ for retrieving the management port.

The @LocalPort annotation is used to inject the main application HTTP port, not the management port used by Actuator.

@LocalPort
private int serverPort;

This will inject the port on which the main application is running, not the management (Actuator) endpoints.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Accessing the Management Port in Spring Boot

1. Configuring the Actuator Management Port

By default, Actuator endpoints run on the same port as the main application. You can configure a separate port for Actuator in application.properties:

management.server.port=8081

Now, all Actuator endpoints will run on port 8081, separate from the main application.

2. Injecting the Management Port in a Spring Component

Using @LocalManagementPort (Recommended)

import org.springframework.boot.actuate.autoconfigure.web.server.LocalManagementPort;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class ManagementPortBean {

    @LocalManagementPort
    private int managementPort;

    public int getManagementPort() {
        return managementPort;
    }
}

This is the most reliable way to access the management port since it is directly supported by Spring Boot Actuator.

Using @Value Annotation

If you are unable to use @LocalManagementPort, an alternative approach is:

import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Value;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class ManagementPortBean {

    @Value("${local.management.port}")
    private int managementPort;

    public int getManagementPort() {
        return managementPort;
    }
}

This method works but relies on manually retrieving the property.

3. Using @LocalPort for Main Application Port

If you need the port where the main application is running, use:

import org.springframework.boot.web.server.LocalServerPort;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class ApplicationPortBean {

    @LocalServerPort
    private int applicationPort;

    public int getApplicationPort() {
        return applicationPort;
    }
}

This will return the main HTTP port, not the management port.

Best Practices

✅ Use @LocalManagementPort when running tests or needing to reference the management port dynamically.
✅ Use @Value("${local.management.port}") only if @LocalManagementPort is not available.
✅ Use @LocalPort to get the main application port, not the management port.
✅ Always define management.server.port in application.properties if Actuator should run on a separate port.

results.question_plain: Which annotation can be used to inject the Spring Boot Actuator port value in a field used in a Spring Boot class?

prompt.question: _____ means that transactions are completely independent from one another
feedbacks:
Isolation ensures that each transaction executes independently of others, preventing issues like dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads. Transactions do not interfere with one another, even when executed concurrently.

  • In Spring, isolation levels are defined using the @Transactional annotation, for example:

    @Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public void processTransaction() {
    // Transaction logic
}

  • Isolation levels control how transaction visibility is handled in databases.
Atomicity ensures that a transaction is all-or-nothing—if one part of the transaction fails, the entire transaction is rolled back. It does not guarantee independence from other transactions.
Durability ensures that once a transaction is committed, its changes are permanently saved in the database, even in case of a system crash. It does not affect transaction independence.
Consistency ensures that a transaction brings the database from one valid state to another. It does not refer to transactions being independent.
prompt.explanation:
eneral Guide to Transaction Isolation in Spring and Databases

1. What is Transaction Isolation?

Transaction isolation defines how transactions interact with each other when executed concurrently. It determines whether changes made by one transaction are visible to others before they are committed.

2. Transaction Isolation Levels in Spring

Spring provides five isolation levels, each preventing different types of concurrency issues:

  1. DEFAULT – Uses the database’s default isolation level.

  2. READ_UNCOMMITTED – Allows dirty reads (unsafe).

  3. READ_COMMITTED – Prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads.

  4. REPEATABLE_READ – Prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads but allows phantom reads.

  5. SERIALIZABLE – Fully isolates transactions but reduces performance.

3. Setting Transaction Isolation in Spring

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.REPEATABLE_READ)
public void processPayment() {
    // Business logic
}

  • The isolation parameter controls transaction isolation.

  • Higher isolation levels provide more consistency but may reduce performance.

4. Best Practices for Managing Transactions

✅ Use READ_COMMITTED as a balance between consistency and performance.
✅ Use SERIALIZABLE only when full isolation is required.
✅ Ensure transactions are short-lived to avoid locking issues.
✅ Use optimistic locking for high-concurrency applications.

results.question_plain: _____ means that transactions are completely independent from one another

prompt.question:
Which of the following annotations is best described by the text below?
It defines class-level metadata that is used to determine how to load and configure an ApplicationContext for integration tests.
feedbacks:
The @ContextConfiguration annotation defines how the Spring test framework loads and configures the ApplicationContext for an integration test.

  • It allows specifying configuration classes, XML configuration files, or even component scanning for setting up the test environment.

  • Example usage:

    @RunWith(SpringRunner.class)
@ContextConfiguration(classes = AppConfig.class)
public class MyIntegrationTest {
    @Autowired
    private MyService myService;

    @Test
    public void testService() {
        assertNotNull(myService);
    }
}

  • This ensures that the test runs within a Spring-managed environment.

This annotation is used to override properties for a test environment. It does not determine how to load the ApplicationContext.

@TestPropertySource(locations = "classpath:test.properties")

  • Used for loading test-specific properties, not configuring the entire context.

The @DirtiesContext annotation marks the ApplicationContext as dirty, meaning it will be reloaded for the next test.

@DirtiesContext

  • Useful when a test modifies the context state, but it does not configure the test context.

This annotation marks a class as a Spring configuration class but does not define how to load the ApplicationContext for a test.

@Configuration
public class AppConfig {
    @Bean
    public MyService myService() {
        return new MyService();
    }
}

  • It is used inside configuration classes, not in test classes.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to @ContextConfiguration in Spring Tests

1. What is @ContextConfiguration?

@ContextConfiguration is a Spring test annotation that tells the testing framework how to load the application context during integration testing. It is often used with @RunWith(SpringRunner.class) to ensure Spring-managed dependencies are available in tests.

2. Ways to Use @ContextConfiguration

Loading a Java Configuration Class

@ContextConfiguration(classes = AppConfig.class)
public class MyIntegrationTest {
    @Autowired
    private MyService myService;
}

  • Loads AppConfig, which defines beans for testing.

Loading an XML Configuration File

@ContextConfiguration(locations = "classpath:applicationContext.xml")
public class MyIntegrationTest {
}

  • Uses an XML file instead of a Java configuration class.

Using Component Scanning

@ContextConfiguration
@ComponentScan(basePackages = "com.example")
public class MyIntegrationTest {
}

  • Automatically scans and registers components.

3. Best Practices for Using @ContextConfiguration

✅ Use Java-based configuration instead of XML for better maintainability.
✅ Keep test configurations lightweight to improve test speed.
✅ Combine @ContextConfiguration with mocking frameworks like Mockito for isolated tests.

prompt.question: Which of the following are auto-configured when using @DataJpaTest?
links: [Testing JPA Queries with Spring Boot and @DataJpaTest:](https://reflectoring.io/spring-boot-data-jpa-test/)
[Testing the Persistence Layer with Spring Boot @DataJpaTest:](https://www.arhohuttunen.com/spring-boot-datajpatest/)
feedbacks:
Incorrect, JSR-303 (Bean Validation) is not auto-configured by @DataJpaTest.
Although JPA providers like Hibernate support bean validation, it requires explicit configuration and the presence of a validation provider (such as Hibernate Validator) on the classpath.

Correct, @DataJpaTest auto-configures components relevant to JPA tests, including JdbcTemplate.
This allows for direct interaction with the database during testing, enabling verification of database operations alongside repository behavior.

Incorrect, caching is not auto-configured by @DataJpaTest.
This annotation focuses specifically on JPA components such as repositories, entities, and the TestEntityManager, but it does not include caching configurations.

Correct, @DataJpaTest auto-configures a TestEntityManager, which is a specialized alternative to the standard JPA EntityManager designed specifically for testing purposes.
It provides convenient methods for persisting, flushing, and finding entities during tests.

Example:

@Autowired
private TestEntityManager entityManager;

@Test
public void testSaveUser() {
    User user = new User("John");
    entityManager.persistAndFlush(user);
    assertNotNull(user.getId());
}
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to @DataJpaTest in Spring Boot

Purpose of @DataJpaTest

@DataJpaTest is a specialized test annotation in Spring Boot designed to test JPA repositories and related components. It configures an in-memory database, scans for @Entity classes, and sets up Spring Data JPA repositories for testing purposes.

Auto-Configured Components with @DataJpaTest

When using @DataJpaTest, Spring Boot automatically configures the following components:​

  • In-Memory Embedded Database: If an embedded database like H2 is present in the classpath, it is auto-configured for testing.

  • Entity Scanning: Scans and registers @Entity classes.​

  • Spring Data JPA Repositories: Configures repository interfaces annotated with @Repository.​

  • JdbcTemplate: Provides a JdbcTemplate bean for direct database interactions during tests.​

  • TestEntityManager: Offers a TestEntityManager bean as an alternative to the standard JPA EntityManager, simplifying testing.

By default, tests annotated with @DataJpaTest are transactional and roll back at the end of each test, ensuring a clean state for subsequent tests. ​

Limitations of @DataJpaTest

@DataJpaTest focuses solely on JPA components and does not auto-configure:

  • JSR-303 Bean Validation: Requires explicit configuration and a validation provider.​

  • Caching: Not included in the auto-configuration scope.​

  • Database Migration Tools (Liquibase and Flyway): These tools are not auto-configured and need explicit setup if required for tests.​

Best Practices for Using @DataJpaTest

  • Use In-Memory Databases: Leverage embedded databases like H2 for faster and isolated tests.​

  • Transactional Tests: Rely on the default transactional behavior to maintain test isolation.​

  • Explicit Configuration: Manually configure components not covered by @DataJpaTest as needed.​

prompt.question: Which of the following endpoints are exposed by default via JMX, after adding spring-boot-starter-actuator as a dependency to your project?
feedbacks: The logfile endpoint is not exposed via JMX by default. It is typically available over HTTP to provide access to the application's log file.
The beans endpoint, which displays a complete list of all the Spring beans in your application, is exposed via JMX by default.
The metrics endpoint, offering various application metrics, is exposed via JMX by default.
The prometheus endpoint is designed to be scraped by Prometheus servers and is not exposed via JMX by default. It is typically available over HTTP.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Spring Boot Actuator Endpoints and JMX

1. What is Spring Boot Actuator?

Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready features to help monitor and manage applications. It exposes various endpoints that can be used to gather metrics, understand application health, and perform other management tasks.​

2. Endpoint Exposure via JMX

When you include the spring-boot-starter-actuator dependency in your project, many endpoints are auto-configured and exposed via JMX by default. Some of these include:​

  • beans: Displays a complete list of all the Spring beans in your application.​

  • metrics: Shows 'metrics' information for the current application.​

  • health: Shows application health information.​

  • env: Exposes properties from Spring’s ConfigurableEnvironment.​

These endpoints are registered as MBeans in the org.springframework.boot domain. ​

3. Accessing JMX Endpoints

To access these JMX endpoints, you can use tools like JConsole, which is bundled with the JDK. By connecting to your application’s JVM using JConsole, you can navigate to the MBeans tab and explore the exposed endpoints under the org.springframework.boot domain. ​

4. Enabling JMX in Spring Boot

By default, JMX is enabled in Spring Boot applications. However, if it has been disabled, you can re-enable it by setting the following property in your application.properties or application.yml:​

spring.jmx.enabled=true

This ensures that the management endpoints are exposed as JMX MBeans.
