prompt.question: Which of these are true about the Spring IoC container?
feedbacks: The Spring IoC container is not a JEE-compliant server like Tomcat or WildFly. It is a lightweight dependency injection container that manages the lifecycle of beans. It is used to create, configure, and manage application components but does not provide the full range of services a JEE server does.

The Spring IoC (Inversion of Control) container is responsible for managing bean creation, dependency injection, and lifecycle management.
Example of a Spring bean managed by the container:

@Component
public class UserService {
    public void registerUser() {
        System.out.println("User registered!");
    }
}

The Spring container automatically creates an instance of UserService and manages its lifecycle.

The ApplicationContext interface is a central component of the Spring IoC container. It extends BeanFactory and provides additional features such as:

  • Bean lifecycle management

  • Event propagation

  • Internationalization support

Example of initializing an IoC container using ApplicationContext:

AnnotationConfigApplicationContext context = new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext(AppConfig.class);
UserService userService = context.getBean(UserService.class);
userService.registerUser();

Here, AnnotationConfigApplicationContext is an implementation of ApplicationContext, and it manages beans defined in AppConfig

The Spring IoC container uses metadata to configure beans. The configuration can be done using:

  • XML-based configuration:

    <bean id="userService" class="com.example.UserService"/>

  • Java-based configuration using @Configuration:

    @Configuration
public class AppConfig {
    @Bean
    public UserService userService() {
        return new UserService();
    }
}

  • Annotation-based configuration using @ComponentScan:

    @Component
public class UserService {
}
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to the Spring IoC Container

1. What is the IoC Container?

The Inversion of Control (IoC) container is at the core of the Spring Framework. It manages the lifecycles, dependencies, and configurations of application objects (beans).

2. Key Components of the Spring IoC Container

  • BeanFactory → The simplest IoC container, used for lightweight applications.

  • ApplicationContext → A more advanced container that supports enterprise features like event propagation and internationalization.

3. Types of Spring IoC Containers

  • AnnotationConfigApplicationContext – For Java-based configuration using annotations.

  • ClassPathXmlApplicationContext – For XML-based configuration.

  • GenericWebApplicationContext – Used in web applications.

4. How the Spring IoC Container Works

  1. Loads Configuration Metadata – Reads bean definitions from XML, Java annotations, or Java configuration.

  2. Instantiates Beans – Creates objects (beans) based on metadata.

  3. Injects Dependencies – Performs dependency injection to wire beans together.

  4. Manages Bean Lifecycle – Handles initialization, destruction, and scope management.

5. Best Practices for Using the IoC Container

✅ Prefer @Component and @Bean annotations over XML configuration.
✅ Use ApplicationContext instead of BeanFactory for enterprise applications.
✅ Avoid unnecessary singleton beans that hold state.
✅ Use dependency injection instead of manually creating objects inside classes.
answers: It is a JEE-compliant (Java Platform, Enterprise Edition) server implementation.
It is responsible for instantiating, configuring, and assembling Spring beans
ApplicationContext is a representation of the Spring IoC container
It gets its instructions on the beans it needs to work with by reading configuration metadata. The metadata can be in XML, Java annotations, or Java code.

results.question_plain: Which of these are true about the Spring IoC container?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are correct regarding methods annotated with the @PostConstruct annotation?
feedbacks: The @PostConstruct method executes only once in the bean lifecycle. It runs after the bean is created and dependencies are injected, but before the bean is used.

A @PostConstruct method must not be private, static, or final. It should have a public or protected visibility to allow the Spring container to invoke it properly.

  • Incorrect usage:

    @PostConstruct
private void init() { // Will cause an error
    System.out.println("Bean initialized");
}

  • Correct usage:

    @PostConstruct
public void init() {
    System.out.println("Bean initialized");
}
The @PostConstruct method cannot be final. If marked as final, it will cause an error because Spring or CDI frameworks might need to proxy the bean and override its methods.

The @PostConstruct method is executed after dependency injection is complete, as part of the bean initialization phase. It runs within the container's singleton creation lock, ensuring the bean is properly initialized. According to Spring documentation, the bean is only considered fully initialized and ready to be published to other beans after the @PostConstruct method completes. This makes @PostConstructideal for initialization logic that requires injected dependencies.
prompt.explanation:
1. What Is @PostConstruct

The @PostConstruct annotation marks a method to be executed after dependency injection is complete, as part of the bean initialization process.
It runs inside the container’s singleton creation lock, ensuring thread-safe initialization.

Bean Lifecycle Order:

  1. Bean construction (constructor call)

  2. Dependency injection (@Autowired, @Value)

  3. @PostConstruct method execution

  4. Bean is fully initialized and ready for use

2. How to Use @PostConstruct

import jakarta.annotation.PostConstruct;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

@Component
public class MyBean {
    
    @Autowired
    private SomeService service;
    
    @PostConstruct
    public void init() {
        // Dependencies are already injected here
        System.out.println("Bean initialized with service: " + service);
    }
}

The init() method is executed once, after dependencies are injected but before the bean becomes available to other beans.

3. Common Use Cases of @PostConstruct

  • Initializing resources using injected dependencies

  • Validating that required dependencies are present

  • Setting up caches or connection pools

  • Loading configuration data

Example:

@PostConstruct
public void setup() {
    if (this.repository == null) {
        throw new IllegalStateException("Repository is required!");
    }
    this.cache = repository.loadCache();
}

4. Thread Safety

@PostConstruct methods execute within the container's singleton creation lock.
According to Spring documentation:

“The bean instance is only considered fully initialized and ready to be published to others after returning from the @PostConstruct method.”

This ensures that initialization logic is thread-safe and executed only once.

5. Alternative to @PostConstruct in Spring Boot 3+

Since Spring Boot 3 migrated to Jakarta EE, make sure to use:

import jakarta.annotation.PostConstruct;

instead of:

import javax.annotation.PostConstruct;

Alternatively, you can implement the InitializingBean interface:

@Component
public class MyBean implements InitializingBean {
    @Override
    public void afterPropertiesSet() {
        System.out.println("Bean initialized");
    }
}

Best Practices for Using @PostConstruct

  • Use @PostConstruct for setup logic that depends on injected dependencies

  • The method should be public or protected (not private, static, or final)

  • Avoid heavy operations to prevent slow startup

  • The method can throw checked exceptions

  • In Spring Boot 3+, always use jakarta.annotation.PostConstruct
answers: A method annotated with @PostConstruct is called only once
A method that is annotated with @PostConstruct can't be private
A method that is annotated with @PostConstruct must be final
Method annotated with @PostConstruct is called after dependency injection is done

prompt.question:
Which of the following is the valid order of methods execution during a bean's lifecycle?


1) @PostConstruct annotated methods
2) @PreDestroy annotated methods
3) BeanPostProcessor::postProcessAfterInitialization
4) BeanPostProcessor::postProcessBeforeInitialization


Select the correct order of their execution
feedbacks:
Incorrect because @PreDestroy (2) should be last, not first.

BeanPostProcessor::postProcessBeforeInitialization (4) must occur before @PostConstruct (1).

Incorrect because postProcessAfterInitialization (3) comes after @PostConstruct, not before.

@PreDestroy (2) should be last, not before @PostConstruct.

The correct execution order of methods during a Spring bean's lifecycle is:

  1. BeanPostProcessor::postProcessBeforeInitialization

    • This method is executed before any initialization logic runs.

    • It allows modifying the bean instance before Spring applies further initialization.

  2. @PostConstruct annotated methods

    • Methods annotated with @PostConstruct run after the bean has been created and dependencies injected.

    • Typically used for performing setup logic.

  3. BeanPostProcessor::postProcessAfterInitialization

    • This method is executed after the bean has been fully initialized.

    • Allows further modifications to the bean before it is used.

  4. @PreDestroy annotated methods

    • Methods annotated with @PreDestroy execute before the bean is removed from the Spring context.

    • Typically used for cleanup logic, such as closing connections.

Incorrect because @PreDestroy (2) must be last, not second.

BeanPostProcessor::postProcessBeforeInitialization (4) should come before @PostConstruct (1), but here it's ordered incorrectly.
prompt.explanation:
Spring provides a bean lifecycle that allows developers to hook into different phases of bean creation, initialization, and destruction.

Key Phases in the Bean Lifecycle:

  1. Instantiation

    • The Spring container creates an instance of the bean.

  2. Dependency Injection

    • Spring injects dependencies into the bean.

  3. Pre-Initialization (BeanPostProcessor::postProcessBeforeInitialization)

    • This method is executed before the initialization process.

  4. Initialization (@PostConstruct)

    • Methods annotated with @PostConstruct execute after dependency injection.

  5. Post-Initialization (BeanPostProcessor::postProcessAfterInitialization)

    • The bean is modified (if needed) after initialization.

  6. Destruction (@PreDestroy)

    • The bean executes cleanup logic before being removed.

5. Code Example Demonstrating Bean Lifecycle

Below is an example demonstrating how Spring executes these lifecycle methods:

mport org.springframework.beans.factory.config.BeanPostProcessor;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import javax.annotation.PostConstruct;
import javax.annotation.PreDestroy;

@Component
public class MyBean {
    
    public MyBean() {
        System.out.println("Bean Constructor: Instantiating MyBean");
    }

    @PostConstruct
    public void init() {
        System.out.println("@PostConstruct: Initializing MyBean");
    }

    @PreDestroy
    public void destroy() {
        System.out.println("@PreDestroy: Cleaning up MyBean");
    }
}

@Component
class MyBeanPostProcessor implements BeanPostProcessor {

    @Override
    public Object postProcessBeforeInitialization(Object bean, String beanName) {
        System.out.println("BeanPostProcessor::postProcessBeforeInitialization - " + beanName);
        return bean;
    }

    @Override
    public Object postProcessAfterInitialization(Object bean, String beanName) {
        System.out.println("BeanPostProcessor::postProcessAfterInitialization - " + beanName);
        return bean;
    }
}

Expected Output in Order:

Bean Constructor: Instantiating MyBean
BeanPostProcessor::postProcessBeforeInitialization - myBean
@PostConstruct: Initializing MyBean
BeanPostProcessor::postProcessAfterInitialization - myBean
... (Application Runs)
@PreDestroy: Cleaning up MyBean

This confirms that:

  • postProcessBeforeInitialization is called before @PostConstruct.

  • postProcessAfterInitialization is called after @PostConstruct.

  • @PreDestroy is executed before the bean is destroyed.

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are correct regarding the @Transactional annotation?
feedbacks:
The @Transactional annotation can be applied at both method and class levels.

  • When used on methods, it ensures that the method runs inside a transaction.

  • When used on classes, it applies to all methods inside that class.

Example of method-level usage:

@Transactional
public void updateAccountBalance(Long accountId, BigDecimal amount) {
    accountRepository.updateBalance(accountId, amount);
}

Example of class-level usage (applies to all methods in the class):

@Service
@Transactional
public class AccountService {
    public void transferMoney(Long fromAccountId, Long toAccountId, BigDecimal amount) {
        // Transactional method
    }
}

The @Transactional annotation allows specifying exceptions that trigger a rollback using the rollbackFor and noRollbackFor attributes.

Example of rollback for a specific exception:

@Transactional(rollbackFor = CustomException.class)
public void processTransaction() throws CustomException {
    throw new CustomException("Transaction failed");
}

By default, Spring rolls back only on unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException and Error).
Checked exceptions (Exception and subclasses) do not trigger a rollback unless explicitly specified.

• Spring by default rolls back only on unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException, Error).

  • Checked exceptions (Exception) do not trigger rollback unless explicitly specified.

  • This can be controlled using the rollbackFor parameter.

Example where rollback does NOT occur (unless specified):

@Transactional
public void processTransaction() throws IOException { // Checked exception
    throw new IOException("This will NOT cause rollback");
}

  • To enforce rollback on checked exceptions, use:

    @Transactional(rollbackFor = IOException.class)

• @Transactional can be applied to classes.

  • When applied to a class, it applies to all public methods inside the class by default.

  • Private methods and static methods are not affected by @Transactional.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to @Transactional in Spring

1. What is @Transactional?

The @Transactional annotation is used in Spring applications to define transactional boundaries. It ensures that a method or class runs within a transaction, allowing rollback and commit operations.

2. Key Features of @Transactional

  • Ensures atomicity – multiple database operations within a transaction succeed or fail together.

  • Rolls back on runtime exceptions – by default, only unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException) trigger rollback.

  • Can specify rollback behavior – allows defining specific exceptions that should or should not trigger rollback.

  • Supports propagation and isolation levels – allows controlling transaction propagation and concurrency behavior.

3. Common @Transactional Attributes

  • rollbackFor – Specifies exception types that should trigger rollback.

    @Transactional(rollbackFor = IOException.class)

  • noRollbackFor – Specifies exception types that should not trigger rollback.

    @Transactional(noRollbackFor = NullPointerException.class)

  • isolation – Defines isolation level (e.g., READ_COMMITTED, SERIALIZABLE).

    @Transactional(isolation = Isolation.READ_COMMITTED)

  • propagation – Defines how transactions propagate when called inside another transaction.

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)

4. Best Practices for Using @Transactional

✅ Use @Transactional on service layer methods, not on controllers.
✅ Do not use it on private or static methods (Spring does not manage those).
✅ Explicitly define rollback rules for checked exceptions if needed.
✅ Use @Transactional(readOnly = true) for read-only operations to improve performance.
✅ Avoid opening transactions in loops, as this can degrade performance.
answers: @Transactional can be used on top of methods
@Transactional allows defining exception types which will cause rollback
By default @Transactional method will rollback in a case of any exception - checked or unchecked
@Transactional can't be used on top of classes

prompt.question: Which of the following Spring Boot Actuator endpoints are considered sensitive by default due to the nature of the information they expose?
feedbacks: The /info endpoint displays arbitrary application information. By default, it is not considered sensitive and is often exposed without requiring authentication. However, if it exposes sensitive data, it's advisable to secure it.

The /health endpoint shows application health information. By default, it provides a simple 'status' when accessed over an unauthenticated connection and full message details when authenticated. Therefore, it is not considered sensitive.
The /httptrace endpoint provides information about HTTP trace data, including request and response details. This data can contain sensitive information, making the endpoint sensitive.

The /metrics endpoint exposes various application metrics, such as memory usage, system load, request counts, and other performance data.
Because these metrics can include sensitive operational information, the endpoint is considered sensitive and should be secured to prevent unauthorized access.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Spring Boot Actuator Endpoints and Their Sensitivity

1. Understanding Actuator Endpoints

Spring Boot Actuator provides various endpoints that allow you to monitor and interact with your application. These endpoints can expose information such as application health, metrics, configurations, and more.

2. Sensitivity of Endpoints

Some endpoints are considered sensitive because they expose detailed internal information about the application, which could be exploited if accessed by unauthorized users.​

Common Sensitive Endpoints:

  • /env: Exposes properties from Spring’s ConfigurableEnvironment, which may include sensitive configuration details.

  • /configprops: Displays a collated list of all @ConfigurationProperties, potentially revealing sensitive configuration properties.

  • /heapdump: Provides a heap dump of the application, which can contain sensitive data present in memory.

  • /loggers: Allows viewing and modifying the logging levels of the application, which could be misused if not secured.​

  • /threaddump: Performs a thread dump, exposing details about the current threads, which might include sensitive information.

Non-Sensitive Endpoints:

  • /health: Shows application health information. By default, it provides basic status information and is not considered sensitive.

  • /info: Displays arbitrary application information. By default, it is not considered sensitive..

3. Securing Sensitive Endpoints

To protect sensitive endpoints, consider the following best practices:​

  • Require Authentication: Ensure that sensitive endpoints are accessible only to authenticated users.​

  • Restrict Access: Limit access to sensitive endpoints based on user roles or IP addresses.

  • Disable Unnecessary Endpoints: If certain endpoints are not needed, disable them to reduce the attack surface.

  • Use HTTPS: Encrypt data in transit by configuring your application to use HTTPS.​

4. Configuring Endpoint Exposure

In your application.properties or application.yml, you can configure which endpoints are exposed and their sensitivity:​

management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info
management.endpoint.health.show-details=when_authorized

In this example, only the /health and /info endpoints are exposed over HTTP, and detailed health information is shown only to authorized users.

Further Reading & References

  1. Spring Boot Reference Guide - Endpoints:

  2. Securing Spring Boot Actuator Endpoints:

  3. Spring Boot Actuator: Production-ready Features:

By understanding the sensitivity of various Actuator endpoints and implementing appropriate security measures, you can ensure that your Spring Boot application remains secure while still benefiting from the monitoring and management features provided by Actuator.

prompt.question: Which answers are true regarding  `update` method in JdbcTemplate?
feedbacks:
The JdbcTemplate methods wrap SQL exceptions inside a DataAccessException, which is a Spring-specific unchecked exception.

try {
    jdbcTemplate.update("DELETE FROM users WHERE id = ?", 1);
} catch (DataAccessException ex) {
    System.out.println("Exception caught: " + ex.getMessage());
}

The JdbcTemplate.execute() method can execute DDL statements, such as CREATE TABLE and ALTER TABLE.

jdbcTemplate.execute("CREATE TABLE users (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(50))");

However, JdbcTemplate.update() is primarily designed for DML (Data Manipulation Language) operations like INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE.

The JdbcTemplate.update() method is used for modifying data in the database, including inserting, updating, and deleting records.

String sql = "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)";
jdbcTemplate.update(sql, "John Doe", "john@example.com");

By default, Spring's JdbcTemplate does not handle transactions automatically.

  • If a transaction manager is configured, JdbcTemplate will participate in an existing transaction.

  • If no transaction manager is defined, each statement runs in autocommit mode (unless explicitly managed within a transaction).

@Transactional
public void transferMoney(Long fromAccountId, Long toAccountId, BigDecimal amount) {
    jdbcTemplate.update("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - ? WHERE id = ?", amount, fromAccountId);
    jdbcTemplate.update("UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + ? WHERE id = ?", amount, toAccountId);
}

  • Without @Transactional, each update() would commit immediately.
prompt.explanation:
Practical Guide to JdbcTemplate in Spring Boot

1. What is JdbcTemplate?

JdbcTemplate is a core part of Spring’s JDBC abstraction layer that simplifies database interactions by handling resource management, exception handling, and query execution. It provides a cleaner way to execute SQL statements without manually managing Connection, PreparedStatement, and ResultSet objects.

2. Why Use JdbcTemplate?

✅ Eliminates Boilerplate Code – No need to write explicit try-catch-finally blocks to manage database connections.
✅ Automatic Exception Handling – Converts checked SQL exceptions (SQLException) into unchecked exceptions (DataAccessException).
✅ Efficient Resource Management – Automatically closes database resources after execution.
✅ Supports Parameterized Queries – Prevents SQL injection vulnerabilities.
✅ Transaction Management Integration – Works seamlessly with Spring’s @Transactional.

3. Setting Up JdbcTemplate in Spring Boot

Spring Boot automatically provides a JdbcTemplate bean if a DataSource is available.

Step 1: Add Dependencies

Include the required dependencies in pom.xml (for Spring Boot & MySQL example):

<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-jdbc</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
    <groupId>mysql</groupId>
    <artifactId>mysql-connector-java</artifactId>
    <scope>runtime</scope>
</dependency>

Step 2: Configure application.properties

spring.datasource.url=jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/mydb
spring.datasource.username=root
spring.datasource.password=secret
spring.datasource.driver-class-name=com.mysql.cj.jdbc.Driver
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=10

Step 3: Inject JdbcTemplate in a Service

Spring Boot automatically creates a JdbcTemplate bean when a DataSource is available.

@Service
public class UserService {

    private final JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate;

    @Autowired
    public UserService(JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate) {
        this.jdbcTemplate = jdbcTemplate;
    }
}

4. Performing Database Operations with JdbcTemplate

Insert a Record

public void addUser(String name, String email) {
    String sql = "INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES (?, ?)";
    jdbcTemplate.update(sql, name, email);
}

Update a Record

public void updateUserEmail(Long id, String newEmail) {
    String sql = "UPDATE users SET email = ? WHERE id = ?";
    jdbcTemplate.update(sql, newEmail, id);
}

Delete a Record

public void deleteUser(Long id) {
    String sql = "DELETE FROM users WHERE id = ?";
    jdbcTemplate.update(sql, id);
}

5. Querying Data Using JdbcTemplate

Query for a Single Object (queryForObject)

public User getUserById(Long id) {
    String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?";
    return jdbcTemplate.queryForObject(sql, new Object[]{id}, new UserRowMapper());
}

Query for a List (query)

public List<User> getAllUsers() {
    String sql = "SELECT * FROM users";
    return jdbcTemplate.query(sql, new UserRowMapper());
}

Using a Custom RowMapper

A RowMapper is used to map rows from the ResultSet to a Java object.

public class UserRowMapper implements RowMapper<User> {
    @Override
    public User mapRow(ResultSet rs, int rowNum) throws SQLException {
        User user = new User();
        user.setId(rs.getLong("id"));
        user.setName(rs.getString("name"));
        user.setEmail(rs.getString("email"));
        return user;
    }
}

6. Using @Transactional with JdbcTemplate

JdbcTemplate works well with Spring’s declarative transaction management using @Transactional.

Transactional Operation Example

@Service
public class AccountService {

    @Autowired
    private JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate;

    @Transactional
    public void transferMoney(Long fromAccountId, Long toAccountId, BigDecimal amount) {
        String debitSql = "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - ? WHERE id = ?";
        jdbcTemplate.update(debitSql, amount, fromAccountId);

        String creditSql = "UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + ? WHERE id = ?";
        jdbcTemplate.update(creditSql, amount, toAccountId);
    }
}

  • If any SQL statement fails, the entire transaction is rolled back.

7. Best Practices for Using JdbcTemplate

✅ Use Parameterized Queries – Avoid string concatenation to prevent SQL injection.
✅ Use RowMapper for Complex Queries – Improves readability and reusability.
✅ Use Transactions for Critical Operations – Ensure consistency in database operations.
✅ Limit Result Set Fetch Size – Optimize performance for large datasets.
✅ Close Resources Properly – JdbcTemplate manages this automatically, but be mindful when using raw JDBC objects.
answers: Just like most of the JdbcTemplate methods, it can throw a DataAccessException
It cannot execute SQL DDL statements
It can execute SQL insert/delete statements
It automatically commits the transaction without requiring any explicit transaction management.

prompt.question: In Spring Boot, which of the following ways can be used to customize an auto-configuration class provided in a Spring starter?
feedbacks:
You can disable an auto-configuration class by excluding it from @EnableAutoConfiguration or @SpringBootApplication.

@SpringBootApplication(exclude = {DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class})
public class MyApplication {
}

This prevents DataSourceAutoConfiguration from being applied.

Spring Boot auto-configuration classes use annotations like @ConditionalOnMissingBean, @ConditionalOnClass, and @ConditionalOnProperty. If you ensure these conditions are not met, the auto-configuration will be skipped.

  • Example: Disabling JPA auto-configuration by not including a JPA dependency or setting a property:

    spring.jpa.enabled=false

If an auto-configured bean is already registered, you can override it by defining your own bean in a @Configuration class.

@Configuration
public class MyCustomConfig {
    @Bean
    public DataSource dataSource() {
        return new MyCustomDataSource();
    }
}

  • Note: In Spring Boot 2.1+, overriding beans must be explicitly enabled using:

    spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding=true
While you can define beans in a @Configuration class, it does not override the entire auto-configuration class itself—only specific beans within it.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Customizing Spring Boot Auto-Configuration

1. What is Auto-Configuration in Spring Boot?

Spring Boot automatically configures beans based on classpath dependencies and application properties. This behavior is defined in auto-configuration classes under META-INF/spring.factories.

2. Ways to Customize Auto-Configuration

✅ 1. Exclude Auto-Configuration Classes

@SpringBootApplication(exclude = {SecurityAutoConfiguration.class})

  • Best for completely disabling specific auto-configuration classes.

✅ 2. Modify Auto-Configuration Conditions (@ConditionalOn*)

  • Example: Prevent DataSourceAutoConfiguration by not defining spring.datasource.url.

spring.datasource.url=

  • Best when you need to disable an auto-configuration feature conditionally.

✅ 3. Override Auto-Configured Beans

  • Define your own bean to replace an auto-configured one:

@Bean
public DataSource dataSource() {
    return new MyCustomDataSource();
}

  • Best when you need to customize a specific bean from auto-configuration.

✅ 4. Use @ConditionalOnMissingBean to Ensure a Custom Bean is Used

  • If an auto-configuration class has @ConditionalOnMissingBean, defining your own bean prevents the auto-configured one from being created.

@Bean
public MyService myService() {
    return new MyCustomService();
}

  • Best for replacing beans without disabling an entire auto-configuration class.

3. Best Practices for Customizing Auto-Configuration

✅ Disable entire auto-configuration classes only when necessary.
✅ Use @ConditionalOnMissingBean to prevent conflicts with auto-configured beans.
✅ Be explicit in overriding beans and ensure they work correctly within Spring Boot's lifecycle.
✅ Avoid overriding auto-configuration manually unless truly required.
answers: Make sure the condition specified by its @ConditionalOn* annotation is false
Override the bean definition (overriding needs to be enabled first)

prompt.question: Which of the following annotations allow specifying access constraints using Spring Expession Language (SpEL)?
feedbacks:
The @PostAuthorize annotation allows specifying access constraints using SpEL and is evaluated after the method execution.

It can validate the return object based on the current user's permissions.

Example:

@PostAuthorize("returnObject.owner == authentication.name")
public Order getOrderById(Long id) {
    return orderRepository.findById(id).orElseThrow();
}

This ensures that only the owner of an Order can access it.

The @Secured annotation does not support Spring Expression Language (SpEL).

It only allows specifying static role-based security using simple role names.

Example:

@Secured("ROLE_ADMIN")
public void deleteUser(Long id) {
    userRepository.deleteById(id);
}

This does not support dynamic expressions.

The @PreAuthorize annotation uses SpEL to enforce security constraints before method execution.

Example:

@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') or #userId == authentication.principal.id")
public void updateUser(Long userId) {
    // Update logic
}

This allows an admin or the user themselves to perform the action.

The @PreFilter annotation uses SpEL to filter input collection parameters before method execution.

Example:

@PreFilter("filterObject.owner == authentication.name")
public List<Document> updateDocuments(List<Document> documents) {
    return documentService.update(documents);
}

This ensures only documents belonging to the authenticated user are processed.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Spring Security Annotations with SpEL

1. What is Spring Expression Language (SpEL)?

Spring Expression Language (SpEL) allows writing dynamic security expressions in Spring Security annotations. It supports logical operators, method invocations, and object properties.

2. Key Security Annotations Supporting SpEL

✅ @PreAuthorize → Executes before method execution
✅ @PostAuthorize → Executes after method execution
✅ @PreFilter → Filters input collection before method execution
✅ @PostFilter → Filters return collection after method execution

3. Best Practices for Using SpEL in Security Annotations

✅ Prefer @PreAuthorize over @Secured → More flexible and supports SpEL.
✅ Use @PreFilter and @PostFilter for collection filtering → Avoid filtering inside methods manually.
✅ Be cautious with @PostAuthorize → Can lead to unnecessary method executions if access is denied.
✅ Use hasRole(), hasAuthority(), and isAuthenticated() functions in expressions.

prompt.question: What are limitations of using the default JDK dynamic proxies in Spring AOP?
feedbacks:
Spring AOP only supports method execution join points, meaning constructor calls are not intercepted.

Constructor interception requires AspectJ, as Spring AOP does not support constructor-based interception.

Example:

@Aspect
public class LoggingAspect {
    @Before("execution(MyClass.new(..))") // This will not work in Spring AOP
    public void logBeforeConstructor() {
        System.out.println("Constructor called");
    }
}

Since JDK dynamic proxies only apply to interface method calls, self-invocation (method calls inside the same class) bypass the proxy mechanism and do not trigger AOP advice.

Example of self-invocation issue:

@Service
public class MyService {
    @Transactional
    public void methodA() {
        methodB(); // This call will NOT trigger AOP interception
    }

    @Transactional
    public void methodB() {
        // Some logic
    }
}

  • Calling methodB() inside methodA() bypasses AOP because it does not go through the proxy.

The number of pointcut designators does not depend on whether JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB are used.

Spring AOP supports the same set of pointcut designators regardless of proxy implementation.

JDK dynamic proxies work only for classes implementing interfaces and can intercept only public methods.

If a class does not implement an interface, Spring falls back to CGLIB to create a proxy.

Example (JDK Proxy Limitation):

public interface MyService {
    void doSomething();
}

@Service
public class MyServiceImpl implements MyService {
    public void doSomething() {
        System.out.println("Executing...");
    }
}

  • Here, doSomething() can be proxied because it is part of an interface.

  • If MyServiceImpl did not implement an interface, JDK proxies would not work, and CGLIB would be required.
prompt.explanation:
General Guide to Spring AOP and Proxy Limitations

1. What is a Proxy in Spring AOP?

Spring AOP uses proxies to apply aspect-oriented programming. A proxy is an object that wraps the target bean and intercepts method calls.

Spring supports two types of proxies:

  1. JDK Dynamic Proxies → Used when the target object implements at least one interface.

  2. CGLIB Proxies → Used when the target object does not implement an interface.

2. Key Limitations of JDK Dynamic Proxies

✅ Works only on interface methods → Cannot proxy a class without an interface.
✅ Intercepts only public methods → Private/protected methods are not intercepted.
✅ Self-invocation bypasses AOP → Calls inside the same class are not intercepted.
✅ Cannot intercept constructors → Constructor calls require AspectJ, not Spring AOP.

3. How to Overcome These Limitations?

✅ Use CGLIB Proxies:

  • If a class does not implement an interface, Spring automatically switches to CGLIB.

  • You can force CGLIB proxying by setting:

    @EnableAspectJAutoProxy(proxyTargetClass = true)

✅ Use AspectJ for Constructor Interception:

  • Instead of Spring AOP, use AspectJ with compile-time weaving.

✅ Use ApplicationContext to Call Methods via Proxy:

  • Fix self-invocation issue by retrieving the bean from the application context:

    @Service
public class MyService {
    @Autowired
    private ApplicationContext applicationContext;

    public void methodA() {
        applicationContext.getBean(MyService.class).methodB();
    }

    @Transactional
    public void methodB() {
        // This will now go through the proxy
    }
}

4. Best Practices for Using Spring AOP

✅ Use interfaces whenever possible → Allows JDK proxies and makes testing easier.
✅ Avoid self-invocation → AOP won't work if calling methods within the same class.
✅ Use AspectJ if advanced interception is required → Constructor interception, field access, etc.
✅ Prefer @EnableAspectJAutoProxy(proxyTargetClass = true) → Ensures CGLIB proxies if needed.
answers: Method calls inside the same class cannot be intercepted.
There are less available pointcut designators.
Only public interface method calls can be intercepted

results.question_plain: What are limitations of using the default JDK dynamic proxies in Spring AOP?

prompt.question: Fill in the blanks. Select the one that applies best. RPC mechanisms like SOAP Web Services are centered on ______. ______ is a popular alternative, and is centered on ______.
feedbacks: JMS (Java Messaging Service) is a messaging technology, not a REST-based alternative to SOAP. It is asynchronous and not used for RESTful communication.

• SOAP and RPC-based services are focused on actions — e.g., createOrder, processPayment.

  • REST is focused on resources — e.g., GET /orders, POST /orders.

  • This aligns with REST's core principle of manipulating resources via HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE).
This choice reverses the correct concepts — SOAP is not centered on “information and resources”, and JMS again is not a REST alternative.
REST is not centered on “actions and processes” — it explicitly avoids that model in favor of resource-oriented design.
prompt.explanation:
1. What is RPC?

Remote Procedure Call (RPC) is a model where the client invokes methods or procedures on a remote service, similar to calling a local method. SOAP Web Services are a common implementation of RPC, especially in enterprise systems.

  • RPC is action-oriented.

  • Communication is often verbose (XML-based), rigid, and strongly coupled.

  • Example:

    <soap:Envelope>
  <soap:Body>
    <createOrder>
      <orderId>123</orderId>
    </createOrder>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

2. What is REST?

REST (Representational State Transfer) is a lightweight architectural style for distributed systems, especially for web APIs.

  • REST is resource-oriented.

  • Uses HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE.

  • Works with representations of resources, typically in JSON or XML.

  • Example:

    GET /orders/123

3. Key Differences Between RPC and REST

  • Design Style:

    • RPC focuses on operations (processPayment()).

    • REST focuses on resources (/payments, /orders).

  • Coupling:

    • RPC is tightly coupled to service contracts.

    • REST is loosely coupled, allowing flexibility in client-server interactions.

  • Standards:

    • RPC commonly uses SOAP, WSDL, and XML.

    • REST uses standard HTTP with JSON/XML payloads.

4. When to Use Which?

  • Use REST when:
    ✅ You want stateless, scalable, and simple HTTP-based services.
    ✅ You're building public APIs, microservices, or mobile backends.

  • Use RPC (SOAP) when:
    ✅ You require strict contract definitions (WSDL).
    ✅ You need WS-Security, transactions, or guaranteed delivery.
    ✅ You're integrating with legacy enterprise systems.
answers: actions and processes, JMS, information and resources
actions and processes, REST, information and resources
information and resources, JMS, actions and processes
information and resources, REST, actions and processes

prompt.question: Which of these is true about @Query when working with database?
feedbacks:
The @Query annotation is used in Spring Data repositories to define a custom query (either in JPQL or SQL) instead of relying on derived query methods.

@Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = ?1")
User findByEmail(String email);

By default, the @Query annotation uses JPQL (Java Persistence Query Language).

@Query("SELECT o FROM Order o WHERE o.status = :status")
List<Order> findOrdersByStatus(@Param("status") String status);

You can specify nativeQuery = true to use native SQL instead of JPQL:

@Query(value = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?1", nativeQuery = true)
User findByEmailNative(String email);
The @Query annotation is part of the Spring Data JPA module, and is commonly used in interfaces extending JpaRepository or CrudRepository.
prompt.explanation:
What is @Query?

The @Query annotation allows you to define custom queries for repository methods. It helps when:

  • The query is too complex to be built using derived method names.

  • You want full control over the query structure.

  • You want to use native SQL instead of JPQL.

How to Use @Query

JPQL Example:

@Query("SELECT p FROM Product p WHERE p.price > :price")
List<Product> findExpensiveProducts(@Param("price") BigDecimal price);

Native SQL Example:

@Query(value = "SELECT * FROM product WHERE price > ?1", nativeQuery = true)
List<Product> findExpensiveProductsNative(BigDecimal price);

Placeholders in @Query

You can use:

  • ?1, ?2 for positional parameters

  • :paramName for named parameters (recommended for clarity)

Return Types

You can return:

  • A single entity

  • A list of entities

  • A projection (DTO interface/class)

  • Optional<T>

@Query("SELECT p.name FROM Product p")
List<String> findAllProductNames();

DTO Projection Example

public interface ProductSummary {
    String getName();
    BigDecimal getPrice();
}

@Query("SELECT p.name AS name, p.price AS price FROM Product p")
List<ProductSummary> fetchProductSummaries();
answers: It allows manual definition of the query to be executed by a repository method.
JPQL can be used with it.
Native SQL can be used with it.
It can be used in Spring Data Jpa.

Yes. SpEL has been supported in XML configuration since Spring 3, typically used with ${...} or #{...} syntax.
Yes. Literal types supported include strings ('hello'), numbers (123), booleans (true), and null.

Operators supported:

prompt.question: Which of these are true regarding the Spring Expression Language?
feedbacks: This is incorrect. While SpEL is inspired by expression languages like OGNL and Unified EL, it is not fully compatible with OGNL and has its own syntax and semantics.

Correct. This is a valid literal expression in SpEL. Single quotes denote string literals in SpEL.
Example:

Expression exp = parser.parseExpression("'Hello World'");

Incorrect. SpEL does support regex using the matches operator.

Expression exp = parser.parseExpression("'abc123' matches '\\w+\\d+'");
Boolean result = exp.getValue(Boolean.class); // true

• Correct. You can register and call static methods as custom functions in SpEL. Example:

    context.registerFunction("reverseString",
    MyUtils.class.getDeclaredMethod("reverse", String.class));
prompt.explanation:
What is SpEL?

Spring Expression Language (SpEL) is a powerful expression language integrated into the Spring Framework. It is used to query and manipulate objects at runtime, access bean properties, perform calculations, define conditions, and more. SpEL is commonly used in annotations, XML configuration, and programmatic expressions.

SpEL was introduced in Spring 3 and is now widely used across Spring projects including Spring Core, Spring Security, Spring Data, and Spring Integration.

Where Can You Use SpEL?

SpEL expressions can be used in multiple places:

  • Inside annotations like @Value, @Conditional, or @Cacheable

  • In XML-based configuration

  • Programmatically using ExpressionParser

  • Inside Spring Security expressions

  • Inside Spring Data query definitions (@Query with #{})

Basic Syntax and Literals

SpEL supports several types of literals:

  • String: 'hello'

  • Numeric: 123, 45.67

  • Boolean: true, false

  • Null: null

Example:

@Value("#{42 + 8}")
private int result;  // Will be set to 50

Property and Method Access

SpEL allows accessing properties and invoking methods on objects or beans.

Assume you have:

public class User {
    private String name;
    public String getName() { return name; }
}

In SpEL:

@Value("#{user.name}")
private String userName;

You can also call methods:

@Value("#{user.getName().toUpperCase()}")
private String upperCaseName;

Operators

SpEL supports:

  • Arithmetic operators: +, -, *, /, %

  • Relational operators: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=

  • Logical operators: and, or, not, !

  • Ternary operator: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse

  • Regex matching: using the matches operator

Example:

@Value("#{5 > 3 ? 'greater' : 'smaller'}")
private String comparisonResult;  // "greater"

Collections and Indexing

SpEL supports working with collections, lists, arrays, and maps.

Example:

@Value("#{myList[0]}")
private String firstElement;

@Value("#{myMap['key']}")
private String mapValue;

You can also filter and project collections:

java
CopyEdit@Value("#{myList.?[length() > 3]}")
private List<String> filteredList;

Registering and Using Functions

SpEL allows registering custom static methods as functions using StandardEvaluationContext.

Example:

public class Utils {
    public static String reverse(String input) {
        return new StringBuilder(input).reverse().toString();
    }
}

// Registering the function
StandardEvaluationContext context = new StandardEvaluationContext();
context.registerFunction("reverse", Utils.class.getDeclaredMethod("reverse", String.class));

ExpressionParser parser = new SpelExpressionParser();
String result = parser.parseExpression("#reverse('spring')").getValue(context, String.class);
// result = "gnirps"

Safe Navigation and Null Checks

SpEL supports safe navigation to avoid NullPointerException using the ?. operator.

Example:

@Value("#{person?.address?.city}")
private String city;

If person or address is null, the expression returns null safely.

Programmatic Evaluation

You can evaluate SpEL expressions at runtime:

ExpressionParser parser = new SpelExpressionParser();
Expression expression = parser.parseExpression("'Spring' + ' Boot'");
String result = expression.getValue(String.class);  // "Spring Boot"

You can also pass a context object:

MyBean bean = new MyBean();
EvaluationContext context = new StandardEvaluationContext(bean);
parser.parseExpression("someProperty").getValue(context);

Common Use Cases in Spring Projects

  • Injecting computed values with @Value

  • Defining conditional bean creation (@Conditional)

  • Writing security access rules in Spring Security

  • Custom queries in Spring Data repositories

  • Dynamically resolving values based on profiles, properties, or runtime conditions

Summary

SpEL is a core skill in advanced Spring development. It allows dynamic behavior, conditional logic, and runtime expression evaluation in configurations, annotations, and code. Mastering SpEL gives you flexibility and power to write more expressive and adaptable Spring applications.
answers: It is fully compatible with OGNL.
Below is a valid Spring Expression Language expression. ‘Hello World’
The Spring Expression Language does not support regular expressions.
The Spring Expression Language supports user-defined functions.

  2. application.properties / application.yml

  3. Profile-specific properties (application-{profile}.properties)

  4. OS environment variables

  5. Command-line arguments

  6. Test-specific sources like @TestPropertySource

  7. Explicit TestPropertyValues programmatically set in tests

This means:

  • If @TestPropertySource is present in a test, its values override anything in application.properties, environment variables, etc.

  • For non-test scenarios, command-line arguments usually have the highest precedence.

Example of using @TestPropertySource:

@SpringBootTest
@TestPropertySource(properties = "my.custom.value=test123")
public class MyTest {
    @Value("${my.custom.value}")
    String value; // "test123"
}

prompt.question: Which of the following are valid transaction propagation values?
feedbacks: This is not a valid propagation type in Spring. There is no PREFERED constant in the Propagation enum provided by Spring Framework. This is likely a made-up or mistyped value.

This is also invalid. The correct counterpart in Spring is NEVER, which means that the method must not run within a transaction. If a transaction already exists, an exception is thrown. NOT_ALLOWED does not exist in the official Propagation enum.

This is a valid and commonly used propagation type. REQUIRED means that if a transaction already exists, the method will run inside it. If none exists, a new transaction will be started. This is the default behavior of @Transactional in Spring.
This is valid. MANDATORY means that the method must be called inside an existing transaction. If there is no active transaction, Spring will throw an IllegalTransactionStateException.
prompt.explanation:
Transaction Propagation in Spring

Transaction propagation defines how a method interacts with an existing transactional context.
It determines whether a method should run within an existing transaction, start a new one, or run without any transaction.

Spring defines transaction propagation behavior through the Propagation enum in the @Transactional annotation.

Valid Propagation Types

1. REQUIRED (Default)

  • If a transaction exists, the method runs within it.

  • If no transaction exists, a new one is created.

  • This is the most commonly used propagation type.

Use Case: Standard transactional service methods.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED)
public void processOrder() {
    // runs in existing transaction or creates a new one
}

2. REQUIRES_NEW

  • Always creates a new transaction.

  • If a transaction already exists, it is suspended until the new transaction completes.

Use Case: Logging or audit operations that should commit independently of the main transaction.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
public void logEvent() {
    // always runs in a new, independent transaction
}

3. MANDATORY

  • Requires an existing transaction.

  • If no transaction exists, Spring throws an IllegalTransactionStateException.

Use Case: Methods that must be executed within an active transaction.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.MANDATORY)
public void updateInventory() {
    // must be called from within an active transaction
}

4. SUPPORTS

  • Runs within a transaction if one exists.

  • Runs non-transactionally if no transaction is present.

Use Case: Read-only operations that can function both with and without a transaction.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.SUPPORTS, readOnly = true)
public Product findProduct(Long id) {
    // runs in transaction if available, otherwise non-transactional
}

5. NOT_SUPPORTED

  • Always runs non-transactionally.

  • If a transaction exists, it is suspended.

Use Case: Operations that should never run within a transaction.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.NOT_SUPPORTED)
public void sendEmail() {
    // always runs outside of any transaction
}

6. NEVER

  • Must run without a transaction.

  • If a transaction exists, Spring throws an IllegalTransactionStateException.

Use Case: Methods that explicitly forbid a transactional context.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.NEVER)
public void performNonTransactionalTask() {
    // throws exception if called from within a transaction
}

7. NESTED

  • Runs within a nested transaction if a transaction exists.

  • If no transaction exists, behaves like REQUIRED.

  • Nested transactions can roll back independently of the outer transaction.

  • Requires JDBC 3.0 savepoint support.

Use Case: Partial rollback scenarios.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.NESTED)
public void processSubTask() {
    // can rollback independently within the outer transaction
}

How to Specify Propagation

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
public void myMethod() {
    // method logic
}

If you don’t specify propagation, Spring uses Propagation.REQUIRED by default.

Common Pitfalls

  • Self-invocation issue: Calling a @Transactional method from within the same class bypasses the proxy, so propagation settings are ignored. Always call transactional methods from another bean.

  • Incorrect propagation choice: Using REQUIRES_NEW unnecessarily can cause performance overhead due to transaction suspension and creation.

Summary

  • Propagation defines how methods participate in transactions.

  • REQUIRED is the default and most commonly used.

  • Use REQUIRES_NEW for independent transactions.

  • Use MANDATORY to enforce transactional context.

  • Use SUPPORTS, NOT_SUPPORTED, or NEVER for non-transactional or conditional execution.

  • Use NESTED for partial rollback scenarios with savepoints.

results.question_plain: Which of the following are valid transaction propagation values?

prompt.question: Which technology is used to accomplish method-level security in Spring?
feedbacks: Incorrect. Spring does support method-level security, and a correct answer is listed among the options.

Incorrect. Servlet filters operate at the web layer, typically used for request/response filtering (e.g., CORS, logging, authentication pre-processing). They do not provide method-level interception, which is necessary for securing individual service or controller methods.

Incorrect. While transactions are applied at the method level (often using @Transactional), they are unrelated to security concerns. Transaction management handles commit/rollback behavior, not authorization or access control.

Correct. Spring Security uses AOP proxies to enforce method-level security annotations like @PreAuthorize, @Secured, or @RolesAllowed. Behind the scenes, Spring wraps beans with security advice using aspect-oriented programming, allowing it to intercept method invocations and perform authorization checks before the method executes.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Security supports multiple ways to secure applications, including:

  • Web-level security with filters and HTTP interceptors

  • Method-level security using annotations like @PreAuthorize, @PostAuthorize, @Secured

Method-Level Security Annotations:

  • @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")

  • @PostAuthorize("returnObject.owner == authentication.name")

  • @Secured("ROLE_USER")

  • @RolesAllowed("ADMIN") (from JSR-250)

These annotations are interpreted through Spring AOP, which wraps the annotated methods in proxy objects. These proxies intercept method calls and execute authorization logic before or after the actual method logic.

To enable method security:

@EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled = true, securedEnabled = true)
@Configuration
public class SecurityConfig extends GlobalMethodSecurityConfiguration {
    // your security config
}

Spring uses proxies (via AOP) around your service or controller beans to apply the security checks at runtime.

results.question_plain: Which technology is used to accomplish method-level security in Spring?

prompt.question: Which class associates a request URL pattern with a list of filters in Spring Security?
feedbacks: This is not the correct answer. DelegatingFilterProxy is a Servlet Filter that delegates to a Spring-managed bean (usually the Spring Security filter chain). It acts as a bridge between the Servlet container and the Spring context, but it does not manage URL pattern to filter chain mappings.

This is a general interface in the Servlet API (javax.servlet.FilterChain), not specific to Spring Security. It represents a chain of filters in the servlet container, but it does not handle Spring Security filter routing or URL associations.

SecurityFilterChain is a Spring Security interface that defines which filters to apply to a particular request, but it does not itself manage the mapping between URL patterns and filter chains. It is used by the component that does — FilterChainProxy.

Correct. FilterChainProxy is the core class in Spring Security responsible for mapping URL patterns to SecurityFilterChain instances. It delegates incoming requests to the appropriate filter chain based on the request path. Each SecurityFilterChain may define its own matchers (e.g., /api/**, /admin/**) and set of filters to apply.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Security uses a filter-based architecture to intercept HTTP requests. The main filter registered with the servlet container is DelegatingFilterProxy, which points to a Spring bean — typically a FilterChainProxy.

Key components:

  • DelegatingFilterProxy: Registered in web.xml or auto-configured by Spring Boot. Delegates to a Spring-managed bean (usually FilterChainProxy).

  • FilterChainProxy: Maintains a list of SecurityFilterChain objects. It matches the request path and forwards the request to the appropriate chain.

  • SecurityFilterChain: Defines a list of filters to be applied for a specific request matcher (e.g., path pattern, HTTP method).

Example Flow:

  1. Request enters the Servlet container.

  2. DelegatingFilterProxy delegates to FilterChainProxy.

  3. FilterChainProxy checks each SecurityFilterChain to see if it matches the request path.

  4. If matched, it applies the list of filters (e.g., UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter, AuthorizationFilter, etc.).

Custom Configuration (Spring Security 5+):

@Bean
public SecurityFilterChain securityFilterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    http
        .securityMatcher("/api/**")
        .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth.anyRequest().authenticated())
        .httpBasic();
    return http.build();
}

Spring automatically registers this configuration inside a FilterChainProxy.

prompt.question: What are the advantages when using @Mock instead of @MockBean in Spring Boot tests?
feedbacks: Correct. @Mock is part of Mockito and can be used to create mock objects for any class, not just Spring beans. This is useful when mocking collaborators that are not part of the Spring application context.

Correct. @Mock can be used as a parameter annotation in test methods when using JUnit 5 with Mockito extensions (@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)). This is not possible with @MockBean, which is meant for Spring-managed beans.

Incorrect. @Mock is provided by Mockito, so you still need the Mockito library on the classpath. It is not available by default unless you are using a test starter like spring-boot-starter-test, which bundles Mockito.

Correct. @Mock works independently of Spring. You can use it in plain unit tests with JUnit and Mockito. @MockBean, on the other hand, requires Spring TestContext Framework, such as @RunWith(SpringRunner.class) or @ExtendWith(SpringExtension.class).
prompt.explanation:
• @Mock is used for pure unit testing, outside of the Spring context.

  • @MockBean is used for integration testing, to replace a Spring bean in the application context with a Mockito mock.

  • @Mock works with any class — not just beans.

  • @Mock can be used without starting the Spring context — making tests faster and more isolated.



    Developer Guide: @Mock vs @MockBean

@Mock – from Mockito

  • Used in unit tests

  • Does not require Spring context

  • Requires Mockito library

  • Typical usage:

@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)
class OrderServiceTest {

@SpringBootTest
class OrderServiceIT {

    @MockBean
    private PaymentGateway gateway;

    @Autowired
    private OrderService orderService;

Use @Mock for fast, isolated unit tests. Use @MockBean when you need to boot the Spring context and mock specific beans during integration tests.

prompt.question: Which database transaction isolation levels allow phantom reads to occur?
feedbacks: Incorrect, Serializable is the highest isolation level and prevents all concurrency issues including phantom reads.
Correct, while Repeatable Read prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads, it still allows phantom reads to occur.
Correct, Read Uncommitted is the lowest isolation level and allows all types of concurrency issues including phantom reads.
Correct, Read Committed allows both non-repeatable reads and phantom reads to occur.
prompt.explanation:
Transaction isolation levels determine how transactions interact with each other in a database system. Phantom reads occur when a transaction re-executes a query returning a set of rows that satisfy a search condition and finds that the set of rows satisfying the condition has changed due to another transaction committing during the first transaction's execution.

Isolation Levels (from lowest to highest):

  1. Read Uncommitted

    • Allows dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads

    • Offers highest performance but lowest data consistency

  2. Read Committed

    • Prevents dirty reads

    • Allows non-repeatable reads and phantom reads

    • Default in many databases (e.g., PostgreSQL, Oracle)

  3. Repeatable Read

    • Prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads

    • Still allows phantom reads

    • Default in MySQL/InnoDB

  4. Serializable

    • Prevents all concurrency phenomena

    • Highest isolation but lowest concurrency

    • May cause significant performance impact

In Spring, you can set the isolation level using:

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.REPEATABLE_READ)

results.question_plain: Which database transaction isolation levels allow phantom reads to occur?

prompt.question:
Given the following Spring component definition:


@Repository
@Profile({"dev", "!prod"})
public class MyRepoImpl implements MyRepo {
    // implementation
}

Which statements about the @Profile annotation usage are correct?
feedbacks: Correct, the bean will be created when the "dev" profile is active, as "dev" is explicitly listed in the profile expression.
Incorrect, this is a valid usage of @Profile annotation. The annotation correctly uses both a direct profile name ("dev") and a profile expression ("!prod").
Incorrect, the bean will NOT be created when "prod" profile is active because of the "!prod" expression, which explicitly excludes the bean from being created when "prod" profile is active.
Incorrect, @Profile can be used on @Bean methods in @Configuration classes as well as on component-level annotations.
prompt.explanation:
The @Profile annotation in Spring is used to conditionally register beans based on active profiles. Profile expressions support several important features:

Profile Expression Rules:

  1. Direct Profile Names:

    • Simple profile names: "dev", "prod", "test"

    • Bean is created when the specified profile is active

  2. Logical Operators:

    • "!" (NOT): "!prod" means "when prod is NOT active"

    • "&" (AND): "dev & test" means "when both dev AND test are active"

    • "|" (OR): "dev | test" means "when either dev OR test is active"

  3. Multiple Profiles:

    • Can specify multiple profiles in an array

    • Profiles are combined with OR logic

    • Example: {"dev", "test"} means "when either dev OR test is active"

  4. Profile Combinations:

    • Can combine direct names with expressions

    • Example: {"dev", "!prod"} means "when dev is active OR prod is NOT active"

Common Use Cases:

  1. Environment-Specific Configurations:

    • Different database settings per environment

    • Environment-specific security configurations

    • Feature toggles

  2. Testing:

    • Mock implementations for testing

    • Test-specific configurations

  3. Feature Management:

    • Feature toggles based on environment

    • Gradual feature rollout
answers: The MyRepoImpl will be created as a Spring bean, when the “dev” profile is active.
It is an invalid usage of the @Profile annotation.
The MyRepoImpl will be created as a Spring bean, when the “prod” profile is active.
