prompt.question: The presence of this annotation informs Spring Boot that it should take an opinionated approach to configuring the application
feedbacks:
Incorrect, @EnableSpringBootConfig does not exist in Spring Boot. This is a made-up annotation that has no equivalent in the framework.
The confusion may come from mixing up existing annotations like @EnableAutoConfiguration or @SpringBootConfiguration, but there is no such annotation named @EnableSpringBootConfig in Spring Boot.

Incorrect, @OpinionatedConfig is not a valid Spring Boot annotation.
While Spring Boot is indeed “opinionated” in its configuration style — meaning it provides sensible defaults and conventions — there’s no annotation called @OpinionatedConfig.
The term “opinionated” describes a design philosophy, not an actual annotation or class.

Correct, @EnableAutoConfiguration is the real annotation that activates Spring Boot’s auto-configuration mechanism.
It tells Spring Boot to automatically configure your application based on:

  1. Classpath dependencies (e.g., if spring-boot-starter-web is present, Spring Boot configures MVC automatically).

  2. Beans already defined in the context (to avoid conflicts).

  3. Configuration properties from application.properties or application.yml.

This is part of Spring Boot’s “opinionated configuration” philosophy — it makes smart decisions for you while still allowing overrides.

Usually, you don’t need to add @EnableAutoConfiguration explicitly because it’s already included in:

@SpringBootApplication

which is a meta-annotation that combines:

  • @Configuration

  • @EnableAutoConfiguration

  • @ComponentScan
prompt.explanation:
@EnableAutoConfiguration — Core of Spring Boot’s Opinionated Configuration

@EnableAutoConfiguration is the heart of Spring Boot’s auto-configuration system. It embodies Spring Boot’s “opinionated” philosophy — configuring your application automatically based on what’s available, while still allowing full customization.

What Is the "Opinionated Approach"?

Spring Boot makes smart, convention-based decisions about application setup:

  • Detects what’s on the classpath (e.g., spring-boot-starter-web).

  • Adapts based on existing beans (avoids duplication).

  • Reacts to configuration properties (e.g., server.port=9090).

  • Provides sensible defaults when nothing is specified.

This approach removes boilerplate, speeds up development, and keeps your configuration clean.

How @EnableAutoConfiguration Works

1. Classpath Scanning

Spring Boot inspects all JARs and libraries on the classpath to determine what features should be enabled.

Example:

  • If spring-boot-starter-web is present → configures MVC, Tomcat, and JSON support.

  • If spring-boot-starter-data-jpa + a database driver is present → configures JPA and a DataSource.

2. Conditional Configuration

Auto-configuration classes rely heavily on conditional annotations, such as:

  • @ConditionalOnClass — activates only if a specific class is on the classpath.

  • @ConditionalOnMissingBean — activates only if no user-defined bean of that type exists.

  • @ConditionalOnProperty — activates only if a property is defined.

These conditions ensure that auto-configuration is applied only when appropriate, and backs off when custom configurations exist.

3. Intelligent Defaults

If you haven’t defined a bean or property, Spring Boot supplies defaults:

  • Default web server port: 8080

  • Default in-memory database: H2

  • Default security: basic login form with generated password

Defaults can be overridden easily by:

  • Adding your own bean definitions.

  • Setting properties in application.properties or application.yml.

Examples of Auto-Configuration in Action

Web Application

  • Classpath: spring-boot-starter-web

  • Auto-configures: Embedded Tomcat, DispatcherServlet, JSON via Jackson

  • Default port: 8080 (override with server.port=9090)

Database

  • Classpath: spring-boot-starter-data-jpa + JDBC driver

  • Auto-configures: DataSource, EntityManagerFactory, TransactionManager

  • Default: in-memory H2 database if no external one provided

Security

  • Classpath: spring-boot-starter-security

  • Auto-configures: UserDetailsService, login form, password encoder

  • Default: all endpoints secured

Usage Patterns

Direct Use (Uncommon)

@Configuration
@EnableAutoConfiguration
public class MyApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args);
    }
}

Via @SpringBootApplication (Recommended)

@SpringBootApplication // includes @EnableAutoConfiguration
public class MyApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args);
    }
}

@SpringBootApplication combines:

  • @Configuration

  • @EnableAutoConfiguration

  • @ComponentScan

Excluding Auto-Configuration

You can exclude specific auto-configurations if not needed:

Annotation-based:

@SpringBootApplication(exclude = { DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class })

Property-based:

spring.autoconfigure.exclude=org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.jdbc.DataSourceAutoConfiguration

Customizing Auto-Configuration

Override defaults by defining your own beans:

@Bean
public DataSource dataSource() {
    return new HikariDataSource(); // replaces auto-configured DataSource
}

Or customize using properties:

spring.datasource.url=jdbc:mysql://localhost/mydb
spring.datasource.username=user
spring.datasource.password=pass

How Spring Boot Decides What to Configure

  1. Scans classpath for known libraries.

  2. Checks if user-defined beans already exist.

  3. Reads configuration properties.

  4. Applies auto-configurations conditionally.

  5. Backs off when manual configuration is detected.

Benefits of the Opinionated Approach

  • Zero Configuration Needed:
    Works immediately with sensible defaults.

  • Rapid Development:
    Get a working app in minutes — no XML.

  • Production-Ready Defaults:
    Logging, security, and metrics are enabled out of the box.

  • Full Control:
    You can exclude, override, or customize any configuration.

Why Other Options Are Wrong

  • @EnableSpringBootConfig ❌ — doesn’t exist.

  • @OpinionatedConfig ❌ — describes philosophy, not an annotation.

  • @AutoConfig ❌ — invalid (missing “Enable” prefix).

  • ✅ Correct: @EnableAutoConfiguration

Related Annotations

  • @SpringBootApplication — Meta-annotation that includes @EnableAutoConfiguration.

  • @SpringBootConfiguration — Specialization of @Configuration for Boot apps.

  • @AutoConfiguration — Used internally by Spring Boot to define auto-configuration classes.

Common Misconceptions

  • ❌ “Auto-configuration is magic and inflexible”
    ✅ It’s fully transparent and can be overridden anytime.

  • ❌ “You can’t control what gets configured”
    ✅ You can exclude or replace any configuration.

  • ❌ “It only works with Spring Boot starters”
    ✅ It works with any classpath library.

Best Practices

  • Use @SpringBootApplication on your main class.

  • Enable debug mode (--debug) to see applied/excluded configurations.

  • Exclude unnecessary configurations for faster startup.

  • Prefer property overrides before custom bean definitions.

  • Review the Auto-Configuration Report in your IDE or Actuator.

Key Takeaways

  • @EnableAutoConfiguration powers Spring Boot’s automatic, opinionated setup.

  • It’s included by default via @SpringBootApplication.

  • It configures your app based on classpath and conditional rules.

  • It’s flexible, transparent, and customizable — the foundation of “convention over configuration” in Spring Boot.

results.question_plain: The presence of this annotation informs Spring Boot that it should take an opinionated approach to configuring the application

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are incorrect regarding Spring field injection with @Autowired?
feedbacks: Spring does not require setter methods to inject values into fields annotated with @Autowired. Field injection allows Spring to set the value directly into the field, bypassing the need for setter methods.
Injected fields can indeed have any access modifier, including private. Spring uses reflection to inject dependencies, making the access level irrelevant.

@Autowired does not use the field name as a default qualifier. By default, it matches beans by type. If multiple beans of the same type exist, you can use the @Qualifier annotation to specify which bean to inject.
@Autowired can be used on fields to achieve field injection. Annotating a field with @Autowired instructs Spring to inject the appropriate bean into that field.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, the @Autowired annotation facilitates dependency injection, allowing the framework to inject bean dependencies into a class. Field injection is one method of achieving this, where dependencies are injected directly into fields.​

Key Points:

  • Field Injection: By annotating a field with @Autowired, Spring injects the required dependency directly into that field. For example:​

    java
KopiujEdytuj@Component
public class MyService {
    @Autowired
    private MyRepository myRepository;
}


In this example, Spring injects an instance of MyRepository into the myRepository field of MyService.​

  • Access Modifiers: Fields annotated with @Autowired can have any access modifier, including private. Spring uses reflection to perform the injection, so the access level does not hinder the process.​

  • Setter Methods: Field injection does not require setter methods. Spring sets the field's value directly, making setter methods unnecessary for this purpose.​

  • Default Behavior: @Autowired performs injection by type. If multiple beans of the same type exist, ambiguity arises, and Spring may throw an exception. To resolve this, the @Qualifier annotation can specify which bean to inject.​

Considerations:

While field injection is convenient, it has notable drawbacks:​stackoverflow.com+1baeldung.com+1

  • Testability: Field injection can complicate unit testing. Without a proper constructor or setter, injecting mock dependencies becomes challenging, often requiring reflection or frameworks like Mockito.​

  • Immutability: Field injection prevents the use of final modifiers on injected fields, hindering the creation of immutable classes.​

  • Design Clarity: Dependencies injected via fields are not immediately visible in the class's public API, potentially obscuring the class's dependencies and violating the principle of explicit dependencies.​

Best Practices:

Due to these drawbacks, constructor injection is generally recommended over field injection:​

  • Constructor Injection: Allows for immutable dependencies and makes the class's requirements explicit.​

    java
KopiujEdytuj@Component
public class MyService {
    private final MyRepository myRepository;

    @Autowired
    public MyService(MyRepository myRepository) {
        this.myRepository = myRepository;
    }
}


In this example, MyRepository is a mandatory dependency for MyService, enforced through the constructor.​
answers: Spring needs setter methods to inject value in a field annotated with @Autowired
Injected field can have any access modifier, including private

prompt.question: Which of the following types of web environments can be specified using the webEnvironment attribute of the @SpringBootTest annotation?
feedbacks: The NONE option for the webEnvironment attribute of the @SpringBootTest annotation specifies that no web environment should be set up for the test. This is useful when testing components that do not require a web server to be running.

The DEFINED_PORT option for the webEnvironment attribute of the @SpringBootTest annotation specifies that the test should use a specific, predefined port for the web server. This can be helpful when you need to interact with a specific port during testing.

The LOCALE_PORT option is not a valid type of web environment that can be specified using the webEnvironment attribute of the @SpringBootTest annotation. This option is not recognized in the Spring Boot testing framework.

The RANDOM_PORT option for the webEnvironment attribute of the @SpringBootTest annotation specifies that the test should use a randomly assigned port for the web server. This can be useful when you want to avoid port conflicts and ensure that each test runs in isolation.
prompt.explanation:
The @SpringBootTest annotation is a powerful tool for integration testing in Spring Boot applications. One of its key features is the webEnvironment attribute, which controls how the application context is loaded and whether a web server is started during tests.


Understanding WebEnvironment Options:

1. MOCK (Default)

This is the default mode when you don't specify webEnvironment. It loads a WebApplicationContext with a mock servlet environment but doesn't start an actual web server. This is ideal for testing @Controller classes using MockMvc. It's fast and suitable for most unit and integration tests that don't require a real HTTP server.


Example:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.MOCK)
@AutoConfigureMockMvc
public class ControllerTest {
    @Autowired
    private MockMvc mockMvc;
}


2. RANDOM_PORT

Starts an embedded web server on a random available port. This is the best choice for full end-to-end integration tests where you need to test actual HTTP requests. Use @LocalServerPort to inject the random port, and TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient for making requests.


Example:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.RANDOM_PORT)
public class IntegrationTest {
    @LocalServerPort
    private int port;
    
    @Autowired
    private TestRestTemplate restTemplate;
}


3. DEFINED_PORT

Starts the server on the port specified in your application.properties (default 8080). Useful when you need to test against a specific port, but can cause conflicts if the port is already in use.


Example:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.DEFINED_PORT)
public class DefinedPortTest {
    @Autowired
    private TestRestTemplate restTemplate;
}

4. NONE

Loads a regular ApplicationContext without any web features. Use this for testing non-web components like services, repositories, or batch jobs where web infrastructure is unnecessary.


Example:

@SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = WebEnvironment.NONE)
public class ServiceTest {
    @Autowired
    private UserService userService;
}


Best Practices:


  • Use MOCK for controller tests with MockMvc (fastest execution)

  • Use RANDOM_PORT for integration tests requiring real HTTP (avoids port conflicts)

  • Use NONE for testing non-web components (lightest weight)

  • Avoid DEFINED_PORT in CI/CD environments to prevent port conflicts


Performance Considerations:

NONE < MOCK < RANDOM_PORT/DEFINED_PORT


Tests run faster with simpler web environments. Choose the lightest option that meets your testing requirements.


Common Use Cases:

• Unit testing controllers → MOCK + MockMvc

• Integration testing REST APIs → RANDOM_PORT + TestRestTemplate

• Testing services/repositories → NONE

• Testing with external tools → DEFINED_PORT (specific port required)


Choosing the right webEnvironment significantly impacts test execution speed, reliability, and maintainability of your test suite.

results.question_plain: Which of the following types of web environments can be specified using the webEnvironment attribute of the @SpringBootTest annotation?

prompt.question: In which of the following transaction isolation levels non-repeatable reads can't occur?
prompt.explanation:
Read committed:
Incorrect, this isolation level does not prevent non-repeatable reads; it only prevents dirty reads. Non-repeatable reads occur when a transaction reads the same row twice and gets a different value each time because another transaction has updated the row in the meantime.

Repeatable Reads:
Correct, this isolation level guarantees that if a transaction reads a row, subsequent reads will see the same data, preventing non-repeatable reads.

Serializable:
Correct, this is the highest isolation level and ensures absolute isolation, meaning transactions are completely isolated from each other, preventing non-repeatable reads, dirty reads, and phantom reads.

Read uncommitted:
Incorrect, this is the lowest isolation level and does not prevent any kind of read phenomena, including non-repeatable reads.

prompt.question: Which answers are true about the customization of bean initializations?
prompt.explanation:
The init() method of the InitializingBean interface can be used.:
Incorrect, there is no init() method in the InitializingBean interface; the correct method is afterPropertiesSet().

InitializingBean is not advisable to be used because it couples your application to Spring.:
Correct, using InitializingBean can couple your code to the Spring framework.

In XML-based configuration, the ‘init-method’ attribute can be used only with void no-argument methods.:
Correct, the 'init-method' attribute specifies a method that will be called on the bean instance during initialization and it must be a no-argument method.

The afterPropertiesSet() method of the InitializingBean interface is where you code your initialization customizations.:
Correct, this method is used for custom initialization logic after all bean properties have been set.
answers: The init() method of the InitializingBean interface can be used.
InitializingBean is not advisable to be used because it couples your application to Spring.
In XML-based configuration, the ‘init-method’ attribute can be used only with void no-argument methods.
The afterPropertiesSet() method of the InitializingBean interface is where you code your initialization customizations.

prompt.question: In Spring Boot, server port number can be changed by configuring in ____________file
answers: By creating a file named server.port in classpath.
By using an annotation @ServerPort in Main Class.
prompt.explanation:
By creating a file named server.port in classpath.:
Incorrect, the server port is not set by creating a file with this name.

pom.xml:
Incorrect, while Maven's pom.xml file is used for project configuration, it is not where you set the server port for a Spring Boot application.

application.properties:
Correct, this is the file where you can set the 'server.port' property to change the default port.

By using an annotation @ServerPort in Main Class.:
Incorrect, there is no such annotation to set the server port in Spring Boot.
